Property ( name = " body " , data_type = wc . DataType . TEXT ), ], ) docs = client . collections . get ( " Doc " ) # 2) Add tenants docs . tenants . create ([ wc . Tenant ( name = " t_acme " ), wc . Tenant ( name = " t_globex " ), ]) # 3) Insert per tenant docs . with_tenant ( " t_acme " ). data . insert ( { " title " : " Acme runbook " , " body " : " ... " } ) docs . with_tenant ( " t_globex " ). data . insert ( { " title " : " Globex incident report " , " body " : " ... " } ) # 4) Query within one tenant only results = docs . with_tenant ( " t_acme " ). query . near_text ( " incident " , limit = 5 ) # 5) Hard delete a whole tenant docs . tenants . remove ([ " t_globex " ]) Step-by-step explanation. multi_tenancy_config.enabled = True on class creation puts every object in a tenant-specific shard. There is no shared shard; cross-tenant queries are explicitly disallowed. Tenants are first-class — you create, list, and remove them via the tenants API. Removing a tenant deletes every object owned by that tenant atomically. Every insert and query must be scoped via with_tenant(...) . Forgetting to scope raises an error rather than silently leaking data — a security-positive design. Cold tenants can be offloaded to disk-only storage ( activity_status = COLD ), reducing memory pressure for tenants that aren't actively queried. Per-tenant backup and restore is supported, so customer-data-export becomes a single API call rather than a custom pipeline. Output. Operation Effect Cost Create tenant new shard initialised < 1 s Insert in tenant normal write, isolated unchanged Query in tenant scoped HNSW traversal normal Remove tenant atomic delete of shard O(1) metadata Cold tenant offload shard on disk only ~ 0 RAM cost Rule of thumb. Multi-tenancy mode is the default for any SaaS Weaviate deployment with > 100 tenants and any compliance pressure. The shard-per-tenant overhead is small until you hit ~100K tenants; above that, look at metadata-filter-based isolation or a per-tier multi-cluster split. Worked example — generative answers with a reranker Detailed explanation. Weaviate's generative modules let you return an LLM-generated answer alongside the retrieved objects in a single query. Combined with a reranker module, you get RAG-quality answers from one HTTP call — no separate orchestration layer. Question. Build a generative + reranker query: retrieve the top-20 by hybrid search, rerank with reranker-cohere , then ask generative-openai to summarise the top 5 in one paragraph. Input. | User question | "What is HNSW and why is it preferred over IVF?" | Code. from weaviate.classes.generate import GenerativeConfig from weaviate.classes.config import Configure # Class already configured with: # reranker_config = Configure.Reranker.cohere(model="rerank-v3.5") # generative_config = Configure.Generative.openai(model="gpt-5-mini") results = docs . generate . hybrid ( query = " What is HNSW and why is it preferred over IVF? " , alpha = 0.6 , limit = 20 , rerank = Configure . Reranker . cohere ( property = " body " , query = " HNSW vs IVF index choice " ), grouped_task = " Summarise the top 5 results in one paragraph for a senior data engineer. " , grouped_properties = [ " title " , " body " ], ) print ( results . generated ) # the LLM answer for obj in results . objects [: 5 ]: print ( obj . metadata . score , obj . properties [ " title " ]) Step-by-step explanation. The hybrid query retrieves the top 20 candidates from the HNSW + BM25 index — fast, broad recall. The Cohere reranker is invoked over those 20 candidates. The reranker scores each against the query with a heavier cross-encoder model, producing a re-ordered list. The reranker's output is the top-5 input for the generative module. Reranking matters because pure HNSW returns "near in vector space" — which is correlated with relevance but not identical. generative-openai is invoked with the top-5's title + body as context, plus the grouped_task prompt. The LLM produces a single summary that answers the user's question grounded in the retrieved docs. The whole pipeline — retrieve, rerank, generate — is one HTTPS call to Weaviate. No separate orchestration layer (LangChain, Haystack) is required for this happy path. Output. Output Value Top-5 reranked titles "HNSW vs IVF — recall study", "When to pick HNSW", ... Generated summary "HNSW is preferred over IVF when... (one paragraph)" Round-trip latency (p50) ~ 800 ms LLM cost per query ~ $0.0008 Rule of thumb. Use Weaviate's generative + reranker modules for prototype RAG. Move to a custom orchestration layer (LangChain, your own glue) when you need fine control over prompts, tool calls, or multi-step reasoning. The Weaviate modules are excellent for "ship in a week." Senior interview question on Weaviate schema design A senior interviewer might ask: "You're designing a Weaviate schema for a multi-tenant docs search product. There are user-uploaded PDFs, blog posts, and chat messages. Walk me through the schema, the vectoriser choice, the tenancy model, and how you'd handle freshness." Solution Using class-per-type + tenant-per-customer + CDC freshness Schema design — multi-tenant docs search on Weaviate ==================================================== Classes (one per content type) - Pdf (title, body, page_count, uploaded_at) - BlogPost (title, body, author, published_at) - ChatMessage (channel, body, sent_at, thread_id) Each class - multi_tenancy_config.enabled = true - vectorizer = text2vec-openai (text-embedding-3-small) - reranker = reranker-cohere - inverted_index on title, body for BM25 Cross-class search - Aggregate query across classes when "search everywhere" - Else scoped to one class for "search PDFs" Freshness pipeline - Source-of-truth: Postgres (uploads + metadata) - CDC via Debezium → Kafka - Worker consumes change events → upsert into Weaviate - For deletes: hard-delete object by primary key Step-by-step trace. Decision Choice Reason Classes one per type distinct properties, distinct reranker prompts Vectoriser text2vec-openai OpenAI's small model — cheap, high quality, multilingual Tenancy per-customer tenant compliance + easy delete Hybrid alpha 0.65 text-heavy domain, lean slightly to BM25 Reranker reranker-cohere better than pure HNSW for top-5 Source of truth Postgres already exists; vector DB is derived Freshness Debezium + Kafka + worker decoupled from transactional path After the decomposition, every schema decision has a defensible reason. The interviewer probes "why not one class with a type field?" — answer: distinct properties and reranker prompts per content type are easier to maintain. Output: Component Value Class count 3 (Pdf, BlogPost, ChatMessage) Tenancy mode enabled, one tenant per customer Average freshness < 30 s after Postgres write Reranker latency overhead ~ 200 ms (worth it for quality) Module stack text2vec-openai + reranker-cohere + generative-openai Why this works — concept by concept: Class-per-type — distinct schemas and reranker prompts per content type beat one big "Document" table. Pulls type-specific knowledge (page_count for PDFs, thread_id for chats) into the property model. Tenant-per-customer — shard-per-tenant gives clean isolation, easy delete, optional cold offload. The right default for any SaaS. Vectoriser inside Weaviate — eliminates the embedding pipeline outside; Weaviate calls OpenAI on insert. Less glue code, less drift between dev and prod. Reranker + generative — RAG quality lives in the reranker, not the ANN index. Generative module turns Weaviate into a one-call RAG endpoint. Cost — write cost is O(embedding API) per object (~ 200 ms); query cost is O(log N × ef) + reranker(top_n) ; tenant overhead is O(1) per tenant up to ~100K tenants. The dominant cost line item is the LLM API calls, not Weaviate itself. SQL Topic — sql Schema design problems (SQL) Practice → Joins Topic — joins Multi-table join drills Practice → 4. Qdrant — Rust-native, ANN-focused qdrant is the Rust-native vector DB — HNSW with optional quantization, payload-aware pre-filtering, sparse + dense hybrid, the lowest-cost-per-vector self-host The mental model in one line: a Qdrant collection is a set of points (vector + payload) stored in a Rust process that puts every primitive — HNSW build, payload pre-filter, quantization, sparse-vector hybrid — under tight, knob-by-knob control, so a self-hosted Qdrant cluster delivers the lowest cost per stored vector at high recall . Once you say "Qdrant is ANN-first, Rust-native, knob-rich," the qdrant interview surface becomes a sequence of consequences from "every decision is exposed and tunable, but you also own every decision." The Qdrant core abstractions. Collection. Named container with a fixed vector configuration ( size , distance ). One collection per logical dataset (per content type or per tenant for strict isolation). Point. A (id, vector, payload) triple. Payload is a JSON document used for filters and stored alongside the vector. Segment. Internal storage unit. A collection has many segments; each holds a slice of points plus its own HNSW index. Segments rotate (similar to LSM SSTables). Shard. Horizontal scale unit for distributed mode. A collection has N shards; each shard owns a subset of points. Cluster mode replicates shards across nodes for HA. Why Rust matters in production. No GC pauses. HNSW queries hit predictable sub-millisecond latency at the tail because there is no JVM stop-the-world. Predictable memory. RAM usage maps directly to vectors × dim × byte-size; no surprise heap growth. Single binary deploy. No JVM tuning, no JNI bridge, no GIL — just a Rust binary on Linux. Operations become "deploy a service" rather than "tune a runtime." Index + quantization options. HNSW (default). Same algorithm as Weaviate / Pinecone. Parameters m , ef_construct , ef exposed at collection-level and overridable per query. Scalar quantization. Each float32 dimension compressed to int8 — 4x memory reduction with ~ 1–2% recall loss. Enabled per-collection. Product quantization. Each vector chunked and quantized — up to 16x memory reduction with 3–5% recall loss. Used when memory is the binding constraint. On-disk HNSW. The HNSW graph itself lives on disk (mmap), only the working set in RAM. Multi-billion-vector deployments at modest hardware cost. Payload + filter expressiveness. Payload is a JSON document — arbitrary nested structure. Filters support must , must_not , should (and/and-not/or) with match , range , geo_radius , geo_polygon , is_empty , is_null , and nested for array-of-object access. Payload fields can be explicitly indexed ( create_payload_index ) — required for any field you filter on at scale; without an index, the filter falls back to a linear scan of payloads. Filters are pushed into the HNSW traversal — the graph walk skips neighbours that fail the predicate. Sparse + dense hybrid (Qdrant 1.7+). A collection can declare multiple named vectors per point: one dense, one sparse, optionally a third for multi-modal. Sparse vectors are token-id → weight maps (BM25-style or learnt SPLADE weights). Queries can search any named vector or fuse multiple via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. This delivers hybrid keyword + semantic search in one engine without a separate Elasticsearch. Replication and HA in self-host. Distributed mode: 3+ nodes form a cluster with Raft consensus for metadata. Each shard has configurable replicas (1–3); writes go through the shard leader, reads can hit any replica. Snapshots can be triggered on-demand or via a periodic schedule, stored locally or pushed to S3. Common interview probes on Qdrant. "Why Rust matters?" — GC-free, predictable latency, single-binary ops. "Scalar vs product quantization?" — scalar = 4x memory, ~1–2% recall loss; product = up to 16x, more recall loss. Use scalar by default; product only when RAM is the binding constraint. "How do you handle filters at scale?" — create payload indexes for any field you filter on; payload-indexed filters are pushed into the HNSW traversal. "Pinecone vs Qdrant for self-host?" — Pinecone is managed (can't self-host); Qdrant is the self-host alternative with comparable recall. Worked example — create collection, upsert, filtered ANN query Detailed explanation. The Qdrant happy path is three operations: create a collection with vector config and any quantization settings, upsert points, search with a payload filter. Compared to Pinecone, you own the knobs; compared to Weaviate, you bring your own embeddings. Question. Create a Qdrant collection for 1,536-dim cosine vectors, upsert 100K points with tenant_id + lang payload, then search with a filter. Input. Field Value Collection docs Vector size 1536 Distance Cosine HNSW m 16 ef_construct 200 Code. from qdrant_client import QdrantClient from qdrant_client.http import models as rest cli = QdrantClient ( url = " http://localhost:6333 " ) # 1) Create collection cli . create_collection ( collection_name = " docs " , vectors_config = rest . VectorParams ( size = 1536 , distance = rest . Distance . COSINE , ), hnsw_config = rest . HnswConfigDiff ( m = 16 , ef_construct = 200 ), ) # 2) Index payload fields you will filter on cli . create_payload_index ( " docs " , field_name = " tenant_id " , field_schema = rest . PayloadSchemaType . INTEGER ) cli . create_payload_index ( " docs " , field_name = " lang " , field_schema = rest . PayloadSchemaType . KEYWORD ) # 3) Upsert points cli . upsert ( collection_name = " docs " , points = [ rest . PointStruct ( id = doc . id , vector = doc . embedding , payload = { " tenant_id " : doc . tenant_id , " lang " : doc . lang }, ) for doc in docs_batch ], ) # 4) Search with filter (pre-filtered HNSW) hits = cli . search ( collection_name = " docs " , query_vector = q_vec , limit = 10 , query_filter = rest . Filter ( must = [ rest . FieldCondition ( key = " tenant_id " , match = rest . MatchValue ( value = 42 )), rest . FieldCondition ( key = " lang " , match = rest . MatchValue ( value = " en " )), ]), search_params = rest . SearchParams ( hnsw_ef = 128 , exact = False ), ) Step-by-step explanation. create_collection registers the schema: vector size, distance metric, HNSW parameters. These are immutable for the collection; choose carefully up front. create_payload_index is the critical step that beginners often miss. Without an index on tenant_id , the filter falls back to a linear scan of payloads — orders of magnitude slower. With it, the filter is pushed into the HNSW traversal. upsert writes points in batches. Each point has an id, vector, and payload. The HNSW graph is updated incrementally; large batches benefit from temporarily disabling indexing ( optimizers_config.indexing_threshold ) then re-enabling. search runs HNSW with ef = hnsw_ef and the filter pushed in. exact = False enables ANN; True falls back to brute-force scan (useful for benchmarking recall). The result is a list of ScoredPoint (id, score, payload). Higher score = more similar for cosine and dot; lower score = closer for euclidean. Output. Metric Value Search latency p50 1.5 ms Search latency p99 4 ms Recall@10 (ef=128) 98% Memory per vector (no quantization) 6 KB (vector + HNSW edges + payload) Rule of thumb. Always create a payload index for any field you filter on; the absence of one is the #1 Qdrant performance bug. Tune hnsw_ef until recall is acceptable; start at 128. Worked example — scalar quantization for 4x memory savings Detailed explanation. A 1B-vector corpus at 1,536 dimensions × 4 bytes = 6 TB of vector data. Scalar quantization compresses each float32 to int8 — 1.5 TB instead of 6 TB. On commodity hardware, that's the difference between affordable and not. Question. Enable scalar quantization on a Qdrant collection, measure recall vs memory, and walk through when it's worth it. Input. Setting Without quant With scalar quant Memory per vector (1536-d) 6144 bytes 1536 bytes Recall@10 (ef=128) 98% 96–97% Code. cli . update_collection ( collection_name = " docs " , quantization_config = rest . ScalarQuantization ( scalar = rest . ScalarQuantizationConfig ( type = rest . ScalarType . INT8 , always_ram = True , # keep quantized vectors in RAM quantile = 0.99 , # outlier clip for better recall ) ), ) # Query — use quantized index for fast first pass, full-precision rescore hits = cli . search ( collection_name = " docs " , query_vector = q_vec , limit = 10 , search_params = rest . SearchParams ( quantization = rest . QuantizationSearchParams ( ignore = False , rescore = True , oversampling = 2.0 , ) ), ) Step-by-step explanation. Scalar quantization clips each float32 dimension to the quantile = 0.99 range, then maps it linearly into int8. The clip prevents outlier dimensions from dominating the quantization scale. always_ram = True keeps the quantized index in memory while the full-precision vectors can live on disk. RAM footprint drops by 4x; disk usage is unchanged. At query time, rescore = True tells Qdrant to fetch the full-precision vector for the top top_k × oversampling candidates and recompute the score. This recovers most of the recall lost to quantization. oversampling = 2.0 fetches 2x the requested top-K from the quantized index for rescore. Higher oversampling = more recall, more cost. Net effect: 4x memory savings on the working set; ~ 1–2% recall loss; ~ 30–50% query latency reduction (smaller working set = better cache locality). Output. Config Memory Recall@10 Latency p99 No quantization 6 KB / vec 98% 4 ms Scalar int8 (no rescore) 1.5 KB / vec 92% 2 ms Scalar int8 + rescore 1.5 KB working + disk 96–97% 3 ms Product quantization 0.4 KB / vec 90–93% 5 ms Rule of thumb. Enable scalar quantization once the working set exceeds 60% of available RAM. Always pair with rescore = True to recover recall. Reach for product quantization only when scalar still doesn't fit. Worked example — sparse + dense hybrid in Qdrant Detailed explanation. Qdrant 1.7+ supports named vectors per point, including sparse vectors (token-id → weight maps). This delivers hybrid search (BM25-like + semantic) in one engine without an external Elasticsearch or a custom score-fusion layer. Question. Define a collection with both a dense (1,536-d cosine) and a sparse (BM25 weights) vector, upsert points with both, and run a hybrid query using Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Input. Vector name Type Source dense cosine 1536-d OpenAI text-embedding-3-small sparse sparse dot BM25 weights from tokenizer Code. cli . create_collection ( collection_name = " catalog " , vectors_config = { " dense " : rest . VectorParams ( size = 1536 , distance = rest . Distance . COSINE ), }, sparse_vectors_config = { " sparse " : rest . SparseVectorParams ( index = rest . SparseIndexParams ( on_disk = False )), }, ) # Upsert with both vectors cli . upsert ( collection_name = " catalog " , points = [ rest . PointStruct ( id = doc . id , vector = { " dense " : doc . dense_emb , " sparse " : rest . SparseVector ( indices = doc . sparse_indices , values = doc . sparse_weights ), }, payload = { " title " : doc . title , " sku " : doc . sku }, ) for doc in docs ], ) # Hybrid query via fusion hits = cli . query_points ( collection_name = " catalog " , prefetch = [ rest . Prefetch ( query = q_dense , using = " dense " , limit = 50 ), rest . Prefetch ( query = rest . SparseVector ( indices = q_sidx , values = q_sw ), using = " sparse " , limit = 50 ), ], query = rest . FusionQuery ( fusion = rest . Fusion . RRF ), # Reciprocal Rank Fusion limit = 10 , ). points Step-by-step explanation. The collection declares two named vector configurations: dense (cosine HNSW) and sparse (sparse-vector index, similar to inverted index). Each point carries both vectors. Dense comes from the embedding model; sparse from the tokenizer + BM25 weighting (or learnt SPLADE weights). The query has two prefetch stages: one ANN over the dense vector (top 50) and one sparse search over the sparse vector (top 50). Each returns its own ranked list. The FusionQuery combines the two lists via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): score(d) = sum over lists L of 1 / (k + rank_L(d)) for a small constant k. The fused list is the final top 10. RRF is robust because it doesn't require score normalisation — it works directly on ranks. The fusion result captures both "exact keyword in the catalog" and "semantically similar product." Output. Method Recall on exact SKU Recall on paraphrase Dense only 60% 95% Sparse only 99% 40% Hybrid (RRF) 95% 92% Rule of thumb. Always run hybrid for catalog / docs / code search where exact terms matter. Pure dense is fine only for free-form open-ended retrieval. RRF is the safe fusion choice — score-weighted fusion needs careful normalisation. Senior interview question on Qdrant scale + ops A senior interviewer might ask: "You need to self-host a Qdrant cluster for a 2B-vector corpus across 4 nodes with HA. Walk me through the cluster topology, the quantization choice, the snapshot story, and the failure scenario for a single-node loss." Solution Using distributed-mode shards + scalar quantization + S3 snapshots Qdrant cluster — 2B vectors, 4 nodes, HA ======================================== Topology - 4 nodes (Rust binary, NVMe local disk, 256 GB RAM each) - Cluster mode via Raft for metadata consensus - Collection sharded into 8 shards, replication factor 2 - Each shard has one leader + one follower; 16 shard-instances across 4 nodes Storage - Vectors on NVMe (per-shard segments) - Scalar int8 quantization, always_ram = true (quantized in RAM, full-precision on disk) - Working set RAM: 2B * 1536 * 1 byte = ~3 TB / 4 nodes = ~750 GB / node (oversubscribed - use product quantization or add nodes) - Full precision RAM: 2B * 1536 * 4 byte = 12 TB total — must be on disk Snapshots - Daily full snapshots → S3 (lifecycle 30 days) - On-demand snapshot before any upgrade - Restore: pull snapshot, replay WAL since snapshot timestamp Single-node failure - Raft detects node failure within ~10 s (heartbeat) - Follower shards on surviving nodes are promoted to leaders - Re-replication starts to restore RF=2 (takes hours, depends on shard size) - Reads continue against followers throughout Step-by-step trace. Phase Action Latency impact Steady state reads/writes across 4 nodes p99 < 5 ms Node fails Raft detects within ~10 s reads continue Promote followers followers become leaders for orphaned shards < 1 s Re-replicate copy data to restore RF=2 hours (background) Node returns catch up via WAL replay minutes Cluster healthy RF=2 across 4 nodes back to steady state The cluster keeps serving reads through the failure with a brief blip during shard leadership transitions. Writes pause for a few seconds while the new leaders take over the affected shards. Output: Property Value Cluster availability 99.95% (single-node failure tolerated) Steady-state p99 latency < 5 ms (warm cache) Recovery RTO seconds (read), seconds (write) Snapshot retention 30 days in S3 Quantization scalar int8 + rescore Why this works — concept by concept: Shards + replicas — shards are the parallelism unit; replicas are the durability unit. Replication factor 2 means losing one node still leaves a complete copy. Raft for metadata — only the cluster metadata (shard assignment, schemas) goes through Raft. Data plane is leader-follower per shard, which scales better than full Raft on every write. Quantization is the cost lever — scalar int8 + rescore gives 4x memory savings with negligible recall loss. Product quantization gives more savings but more loss; pick the level that meets your recall target. S3 snapshots — push snapshots to durable object storage so a whole-cluster loss is recoverable. Daily full + on-demand pre-upgrade is the canonical schedule. Cost — per-shard query cost is O(log N × ef) ; cluster query overhead is O(shards) for fan-out merge. Storage cost scales linearly with vectors and quantization choice; HA doubles the disk cost. Optimization Topic — optimization Query + memory optimisation drills Practice → ETL Topic — etl Self-host ETL pipeline practice Practice → 5. pgvector — Postgres-native pgvector is the "your warehouse already has a vector DB" answer — Postgres extension with HNSW + IVFFlat, full SQL filters and joins, ACID inherited from Postgres The mental model in one line: pgvector is a Postgres extension that adds a vector(N) column type plus HNSW and IVFFlat indexes — every other Postgres primitive (ACID, joins, foreign keys, partitioning, replicas, point-in-time recovery) comes along for free, so for any team where Postgres is already the source of truth, the vector DB is the warehouse . Once you say "pgvector is Postgres + an index," the pgvector postgres interview surface becomes a sequence of consequences from "you inherit every Postgres property — good and bad — including the single-node-write-master ceiling." The pgvector primitives. vector(N) column type. Fixed-dimension floating-point vector stored inline in the row. Operators: <-> (L2), <#> (negative inner product), <=> (cosine distance). HNSW index. CREATE INDEX ON docs USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops) . Parameters m , ef_construction at build time; ef_search at session time. IVFFlat index. Older, lower-memory alternative. Parameter lists at build time; probes at query time. Slower at high recall. hnsw.iterative_scan (0.8+). Allows the planner to use the HNSW index even when a selective WHERE filter would otherwise be more efficient via a B-tree. The "free Postgres dividends" — what you get without doing extra work. ACID transactions. A single INSERT can write a vector, its metadata, and any related rows atomically. No separate "embedding pipeline transactional fence" to design. Joins. A vector query can join with any other table in standard SQL. No external pipeline to fetch enrichment data. Foreign keys. embedding is a column on documents ; referential integrity is automatic. Partitioning. Native Postgres declarative partitioning by tenant_id or date works on vector tables. Read replicas. Streaming replication propagates the vector data + HNSW index changes; read-heavy workloads scale horizontally. PITR + WAL. Same backup story as the rest of Postgres. The scale wall — what pgvector cannot give you. Single-node write master. Postgres has one write primary. pgvector inherits this; if your write throughput exceeds what a single node can do (~10K vector upserts/sec on tuned hardware), you need sharding (Citus, Vitess-for-Postgres) or a different engine. HNSW build is single-threaded historically. pgvector 0.7+ added parallel index build, but still slower than dedicated engines. Memory pressure. HNSW lives in Postgres shared buffers — large indexes need large shared_buffers and large maintenance_work_mem . No native quantization. Float32 vectors only (as of 2026). For 1B+ vectors, the raw storage cost becomes the binding constraint. The hybrid search story on pgvector. Use Postgres full-text search (tsvector) for the keyword side; combine with vector cosine for the semantic side. Standard SQL: ORDER BY (0.6 * (embedding <=> $1) + 0.4 * ts_rank_cd(tsv, $2)) LIMIT k . No dedicated RRF function, but you can implement it in SQL with row_number() over each ranking. Common interview probes on pgvector. "When does pgvector beat a dedicated vector DB?" — when Postgres is already the SoT, corpus ≤ 100M vectors, joins/foreign keys matter, small ops team. "What is the scale wall?" — single-node Postgres write master; sharding is non-trivial. "HNSW vs IVFFlat in pgvector?" — HNSW: higher recall, larger index, slower build; IVFFlat: smaller index, faster build, lower recall at the same speed. HNSW wins for most production cases. "How do you handle freshness in pgvector?" — INSERT inside the application transaction, or use a CDC stream from another DB into Postgres + a trigger that calls the embedding function. Worked example — create extension, table, HNSW index, query Detailed explanation. The pgvector happy path is four SQL statements: CREATE EXTENSION , CREATE TABLE with a vector column, CREATE INDEX HNSW, query with ORDER BY embedding <=> $1 LIMIT k . Everything else is standard Postgres. Question. Set up a pgvector table for 1,536-dim cosine embeddings, build an HNSW index, and run a top-10 query with a metadata filter. Input. Column Type id bigserial tenant_id int lang text body text embedding vector(1536) Code. -- 1) Extension (one-time per database) CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector ; -- 2) Table CREATE TABLE documents ( id bigserial PRIMARY KEY , tenant_id int NOT NULL , lang text NOT NULL , body text NOT NULL , embedding vector ( 1536 ) NOT NULL ); -- 3) Indexes CREATE INDEX docs_tenant_idx ON documents ( tenant_id ); CREATE INDEX docs_lang_idx ON documents ( lang ); CREATE INDEX docs_emb_idx ON documents USING hnsw ( embedding vector_cosine_ops ) WITH ( m = 16 , ef_construction = 200 ); -- 4) Insert (embedding precomputed by app) INSERT INTO documents ( tenant_id , lang , body , embedding ) VALUES ( 42 , 'en' , 'iPhone 16 Pro review ...' , $ 1 :: vector ); -- 5) Top-10 nearest with filter SET hnsw . ef_search = 100 ; SELECT id , body , embedding <=> $ 1 AS distance FROM documents WHERE tenant_id = 42 AND lang = 'en' ORDER BY embedding <=> $ 1 LIMIT 10 ; Step-by-step explanation. CREATE EXTENSION vector registers the type and operators. Available out of the box on AWS RDS, Aurora, Azure Postgres, Google CloudSQL, Supabase, Neon, and self-hosted Postgres 12+. vector(1536) is the column type; the dimension is fixed per column. Distance operators are <=> (cosine), <-> (L2), <#> (negative inner product). The HNSW index supports filter pushdown via hnsw.iterative_scan (default in 0.8+). At query time, the planner uses the HNSW index and applies the WHERE filter to skip non-matching candidates. hnsw.ef_search = 100 is the recall knob, set per session or per query. Higher = better recall, higher latency. The query is just SQL — joins, GROUP BY, window functions all available. The result is sorted by cosine distance (lower = closer). Output. id body (truncated) distance 9213 iPhone 16 Pro review — A18 chip 0.08 9387 iPhone Pro waterproof case 2026 0.15 9050 Best iPhone Pro accessories 0.18 ... ... ... Rule of thumb. Always create a B-tree index on every column you filter on ( tenant_id , lang ). Set hnsw.ef_search per session via SET LOCAL . Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to confirm the planner picks the HNSW index — Index Scan using docs_emb_idx . Worked example — pgvector with hybrid text + vector + standard SQL JOIN Detailed explanation. The killer feature of pgvector is composability with the rest of SQL. You can run a hybrid query that joins the documents table with a documents_meta table, applies a full-text search filter, and orders by a blended score — all in one SQL statement. Question. Write a SQL query that combines BM25 (via Postgres FTS) and vector similarity, joined with a metadata table, returning the top 10. Input. Table Columns documents id, body, embedding, tsv (tsvector), tenant_id documents_meta id, author, published_at Code. -- Setup (one-time) ALTER TABLE documents ADD COLUMN tsv tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS ( to_tsvector ( 'english' , body )) STORED ; CREATE INDEX docs_tsv_idx ON documents USING GIN ( tsv ); -- Hybrid query — vector + BM25 + JOIN WITH ranked AS ( SELECT d . id , d . body , m . author , m . published_at , d . embedding <=> $ 1 :: vector AS vec_dist , ts_rank_cd ( d . tsv , plainto_tsquery ( 'english' , $ 2 )) AS bm25_score FROM documents d JOIN documents_meta m USING ( id ) WHERE d . tenant_id = 42 AND d . lang = 'en' AND d . tsv @@ plainto_tsquery ( 'english' , $ 2 ) -- optional BM25 filter ) SELECT * , 0 . 6 * ( 1 - vec_dist ) + 0 . 4 * bm25_score AS blended FROM ranked ORDER BY blended DESC LIMIT 10 ; Step-by-step explanation. The tsv column is generated from body — Postgres maintains it automatically. A GIN index on it enables BM25-style ranking via ts_rank_cd . The CTE computes both scores: vec_dist (cosine distance, lower = closer) and bm25_score (higher = more relevant). The JOIN with documents_meta is a standard SQL join — joins with arbitrary other tables work the same way. You can also LEFT JOIN, do GROUP BY, apply window functions. The WHERE ... AND d.tsv @@ plainto_tsquery(...) filter is optional. If you want every doc considered for the hybrid score, drop it; if you want at least one BM25 hit, keep it. The final ORDER BY blended ranks by a weighted sum — 60% vector similarity (converted to 1 - distance so higher = better), 40% BM25. Output. id author bm25_score vec_dist blended 9213 jane 0.42 0.08 0.72 9387 mike 0.36 0.15 0.65 9050 jane 0.21 0.18 0.58 Rule of thumb. Use generated tsv columns to keep BM25 indexes in sync. Tune the 0.6 / 0.4 weights via A/B tests rather than offline metrics. Skip the BM25 hard-filter for free-form RAG queries; keep it for keyword-heavy search. Worked example — partitioning a 100M-row vector table by tenant Detailed explanation. At 100M vectors, a single Postgres table starts to hurt: HNSW build time grows superlinearly, VACUUM windows balloon, and any per-tenant operation scans the whole index. Declarative partitioning by tenant_id lets each tenant have its own HNSW index — smaller, faster, easier to maintain. Question. Partition documents by tenant_id hash into 16 partitions, with a per-partition HNSW index. Show the DDL and the query that benefits from partition pruning. Input. Total vectors Partitions Vectors per partition 100,000,000 16 ~ 6.25 M Code. -- Parent CREATE TABLE documents ( id bigserial , tenant_id int NOT NULL , lang text NOT NULL , body text NOT NULL , embedding vector ( 1536 ) NOT NULL , PRIMARY KEY ( tenant_id , id ) ) PARTITION BY HASH ( tenant_id ); -- 16 partitions DO $$ BEGIN FOR i IN 0 .. 15 LOOP EXECUTE format ( 'CREATE TABLE documents_p%1$s PARTITION OF documents FOR VALUES WITH (MODULUS 16, REMAINDER %1$s)' , i ); EXECUTE format ( 'CREATE INDEX docs_p%1$s_emb_idx ON documents_p%1$s USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 200)' , i ); END LOOP ; END $$ ; -- Query — planner prunes to one partition EXPLAIN ( ANALYZE , BUFFERS ) SELECT id , body , embedding <=> $ 1 AS dist FROM documents WHERE tenant_id = 42 ORDER BY embedding <=> $ 1 LIMIT 10 ; Step-by-step explanation. PARTITION BY HASH (tenant_id) splits the parent table into 16 child tables, each holding ~ 1/16 of the rows. Postgres routes inserts to the right child based on the hash of tenant_id . Each partition gets its own HNSW index. Build time is O(N log N) per partition — much faster in parallel than one big index. The query has WHERE tenant_id = 42 , so Postgres' partition pruning kicks in: only the partition holding tenant 42 is scanned. The other 15 partitions are completely skipped. VACUUM, ANALYZE, REINDEX all operate per-partition. Maintenance windows are smaller and parallelisable. Per-tenant delete is a partition truncation (if you partition LIST by tenant) or a normal DELETE within one partition. Output. Metric Single table 16-partition HNSW build time 8 hours 35 min (parallel) Per-tenant query latency 5 ms 2 ms (smaller index) VACUUM duration 2 hours 8 min per partition Per-tenant DROP DELETE + VACUUM DROP PARTITION (instant) Rule of thumb. Partition any pgvector table above ~10M rows. Hash partition by tenant_id for SaaS; range partition by date for time-series. Each partition gets its own index — that is the magic. Senior interview question on pgvector vs dedicated vector DB A senior interviewer might ask: "Your team is on Postgres. You have 50M documents that need vector search for a RAG feature. Walk me through the decision between pgvector and a dedicated vector DB, and explain when you'd switch later." Solution Using the SoT + scale + ops decomposition pgvector vs dedicated vector DB — 50M docs, Postgres team ========================================================= 1. Where is your source of truth? - Postgres already → pgvector inherits the SoT story - No external pipeline, no CDC worker, no eventual consistency 2. Scale envelope check - 50M vectors × 1536 × 4 byte = ~ 300 GB raw - HNSW overhead ~ 30% → ~ 400 GB index + data - Fits on a single 64-core / 512 GB Postgres → yes, pgvector - Above 200M, single-node write master starts to hurt → revisit 3. Filter / join shape - JOIN documents with users, organizations, etc → pgvector wins - Standard SQL filters (tenant, lang, status) → pgvector pushdown - JSONB metadata filters → fine in Postgres - No external lookup → no need for Flink-style async join 4. Ops capacity - Existing Postgres ops team → zero net-new ops - No Postgres team → still simpler than running Qdrant cluster 5. When to switch later - Vectors > 200M - Write throughput > 10K upserts/sec sustained - Need quantization / sub-millisecond cold-cache p99 - Multi-region active-active (Postgres can't natively) Step-by-step trace. Phase pgvector verdict Switch trigger 0 - 10M docs pgvector ideal none 10M - 50M pgvector ideal none 50M - 200M pgvector with partitioning watch write throughput 200M - 1B maybe pgvector + Citus likely switch to Qdrant or Pinecone > 1B dedicated yes — Pinecone serverless or Qdrant cluster The transition point is workload-specific. Some teams stay on pgvector at 500M because their write throughput is low and their filter shape is JOIN-heavy. Some teams switch at 50M because they need quantization and sub-ms cold-cache latency. Output: Verdict Reasoning Start on pgvector Postgres is SoT; 50M well within single-node envelope; zero new ops Plan switch trigger sustained > 5K upserts/sec or > 200M vectors → re-evaluate Migration cost non-trivial — CDC pipeline + dual-write window + cutover Why this works — concept by concept: SoT inheritance — when Postgres is already SoT, pgvector eliminates a whole tier of infrastructure (CDC worker, embedding sync job, eventual consistency). Strongest argument for pgvector. Free joins — pgvector is the only engine where you can JOIN documents with users / organizations / accounts in standard SQL. Every other engine forces a lookup outside the vector query. Single-node write ceiling — pgvector inherits Postgres' single-write-master limit. Sharding (Citus) helps but adds complexity. Above ~200M vectors with high write throughput, the dedicated engines win. Migration path — pgvector → dedicated is a real engineering project (3-6 months for a careful cutover). Stay on pgvector as long as it fits; switch when the scale wall is genuinely hit. Cost — pgvector cost is bundled into your Postgres bill; dedicated engines are a new line item. At small scale, pgvector is dramatically cheaper. At large scale, dedicated wins on hardware efficiency. SQL Topic — sql SQL query practice for pgvector Practice → Joins Topic — joins JOIN drills for hybrid vector queries Practice → Cheat sheet — vector DB picker 5-question picker. (1) Where is your source of truth? Postgres → pgvector; elsewhere → dedicated. (2) What corpus size? ≤ 100M → pgvector OK; > 200M → dedicated. (3) Self-host or managed? Managed → Pinecone; self-host → Qdrant / Weaviate / pgvector. (4) Hybrid (BM25 + vector)? Weaviate or Qdrant native; Pinecone via sparse vectors; pgvector via FTS. (5) Schema + generative built-in? Weaviate. Pure ANN + lowest cost? Qdrant. Zero-ops at any scale? Pinecone. Pinecone serverless 10-line recipe. pc.create_index(name, dimension, metric="cosine", spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud, region)) → index.upsert(vectors=[{id, values, metadata}], namespace) → index.query(namespace, vector, top_k, filter={"tenant_id": {"$eq": 42}}) . Weaviate schema + vectoriser template. collections.create(name, vectorizer_config=text2vec_openai, properties=[Property(...)], multi_tenancy_config=enabled) → insert (Weaviate embeds on insert) → query.hybrid(query="...", alpha=0.65, limit=10) . Qdrant filter syntax cheat. Always create_payload_index on filter fields. Filter = Filter(must=[FieldCondition(key, match=MatchValue(value))], must_not=[...], should=[...]) . For ranges use range=Range(gte=..., lte=...) . For geo use geo_radius or geo_polygon . pgvector HNSW DDL. CREATE EXTENSION vector; CREATE TABLE docs(id bigserial PRIMARY KEY, embedding vector(1536), ...); CREATE INDEX ON docs USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 200); SET hnsw.ef_search = 100; SELECT ... ORDER BY embedding <=> $1 LIMIT 10; . HNSW knobs. m (max edges per node, default 16, 32 for higher recall); ef_construction (build-time candidate list, default 200, 400 for better quality); ef / ef_search (query-time candidate list, default 64, raise for recall). Higher = slower + more memory + higher recall. Filter pushdown rule. Always create an index on every filter field — pgvector B-tree, Qdrant payload index, Weaviate property indexed, Pinecone metadata field included. Without it the engine falls back to a scan over filter values, which kills query latency. Quantization rule. Enable scalar quantization in Qdrant once your working set > 60% of RAM. Always pair with rescore. Use product quantization only when scalar still does not fit (rare). Hybrid alpha rule. Keyword-heavy domains (catalog, code) → alpha 0.5–0.7 (lean BM25). Paraphrase-heavy (FAQ, chat) → alpha 0.8–0.95 (lean vector). A/B test against task-completion metrics, not offline recall. Freshness recipe. Postgres is SoT → Debezium → Kafka → embedding worker → vector DB upsert. Never embed in the transactional write path; an LLM provider outage becomes your write outage. Per-tenant isolation. Pinecone → namespaces; Weaviate → multi-tenancy mode (shard per tenant); Qdrant → collections per tenant (or payload filter); pgvector → hash partition by tenant_id. When to use exact scan. Below ~100K vectors, exact scan in any engine matches ANN latency without the recall trade-off. Add the index only when you cross the threshold. Migration cost. pgvector → dedicated is a 3–6 month project with a dual-write window and a careful cutover. Plan to stay on whichever engine you pick for at least 2 years. Frequently asked questions What is a vector database? A vector database is the marriage of three primitives: an approximate-nearest-neighbour (ANN) index over high-dimensional embeddings, a typed metadata filter engine, and durable storage with a freshness pipeline. You store millions or billions of vectors (typically embeddings from a text or image model) alongside structured metadata (tenant_id, language, date), then run queries like "the 10 most similar embeddings where tenant_id = 42 and lang = 'en' " in single-digit milliseconds. The vector database market in 2026 is dominated by four engines — Pinecone (managed, serverless), Weaviate (schema-rich, open-source), Qdrant (Rust-native, ANN-focused), and pgvector (Postgres-native) — each making different trade-offs across managed-vs-self-host, index type, filter expressiveness, and scale envelope. The most common interview mistake is calling it "a database that stores arrays" — the index + filter combination is the whole product. Pinecone vs Weaviate vs Qdrant — which should I pick? It depends on three answers. Pinecone wins when you want zero operations, predictable usage-based pricing, and a multi-billion-vector ceiling — its Serverless tier decouples storage and compute, so you pay for stored vectors at rest and reads on demand. Weaviate wins when you want a schema-rich, batteries-included engine — typed properties per class, built-in vectorisers (text2vec-openai, multi2vec-clip), hybrid search modules, reranker modules, and generative modules that turn it into a one-call RAG endpoint. Qdrant wins when you self-host and care about lowest cost per stored vector — it is Rust-native (no GC), supports scalar / product quantization for 4–16x memory savings, and ships native sparse + dense hybrid via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. The vector db comparison framework: pick Pinecone for managed simplicity, Weaviate for schema and generative depth, Qdrant for self-host efficiency. Can pgvector replace a dedicated vector database? For corpora up to ~100 million vectors and any team where Postgres is already the source of truth, yes — pgvector postgres is the right answer. You inherit ACID transactions (no separate embedding-pipeline transactional fence), standard SQL joins (no external lookup pipeline), foreign keys, declarative partitioning, streaming replication, and point-in-time recovery — everything Postgres already gives you. Pgvector 0.7+ adds HNSW (alongside the older IVFFlat) and parallel index build, so it competes with dedicated engines on recall and query latency at moderate scale. The scale walls are real: single-node write master limits you to ~10K upserts/sec on tuned hardware, and storage cost grows linearly without quantization. Plan to switch to a dedicated engine when you cross ~200M vectors with sustained high write throughput, or when sub-millisecond cold-cache p99 becomes mandatory. Until then, pgvector deletes a whole tier of infrastructure. What is HNSW and why does it matter for vector databases? HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the dominant ANN index in 2026 — and the vector index hnsw line in every senior interview. It builds a multi-layer proximity graph where the top layer has a few long-range edges and the bottom layer has dense local edges. Queries start at the top layer's entry point, greedily walk toward the query vector, then descend one layer at a time, refining the candidate list at each level. The result: O(log N × ef) distance computations per query instead of O(N) , with 95–99% recall@10 on real workloads. The tunable knobs are m (max edges per node, controls graph density), ef_construction (build-time candidate list, higher = better recall, slower build), and ef or ef_search (query-time candidate list, the main runtime recall knob). HNSW beats IVF on recall at the same query latency, beats brute-force scan by 10–100x in throughput, and is the default index in Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, and pgvector 0.7+. The cost is memory — HNSW adds (m + 1) × N × 8 bytes for the graph on top of the vectors themselves. Hybrid search — vector plus keyword — when should I enable it? Almost always for production search. Pure vector search misses exact-keyword matches (product SKUs, model numbers, proper nouns, code identifiers); pure keyword search misses paraphrase and semantic similarity ("affordable laptop" should match "cheap notebook"). Hybrid search combines a sparse vector (BM25-style or learnt SPLADE weights) with the dense embedding at query time, blending the two scores via a tunable alpha (or via Reciprocal Rank Fusion). Tune alpha = 0.5–0.7 for keyword-heavy domains (catalog, e-commerce, code search, docs); alpha = 0.8–0.95 for paraphrase-heavy domains (FAQ, chat, semantic search). All four engines support hybrid: Pinecone via sparse-vector fields, Weaviate via the hybrid query operator, Qdrant via named sparse + dense vectors with RRF fusion, pgvector via Postgres FTS ( tsvector ) combined with <=> in SQL. The 2026 answer to "should we ship hybrid?" is "yes, by default, then A/B test the alpha." How often should I re-compute embeddings for a vector database? Two rules. Per-document freshness — re-embed any document whose content changes; this is the CDC pipeline (Postgres → Debezium → Kafka → embedding worker → vector DB upsert), end-to-end latency typically < 30 seconds. Never call the embedding API inside the transactional write path — an LLM provider outage would turn into your write outage. Whole-corpus refresh — re-embed every document only when you switch embedding models (e.g., upgrading from text-embedding-3-small to a newer model). Model switches are big events: re-embed in a side index, dual-query during the cutover window, swap traffic to the new index once recall is validated, retire the old index. Day-to-day, embeddings are write-once-per-document; the cost lever is the LLM call, not the vector DB itself. Practice on PipeCode Drill the SQL practice library → for the WHERE / JOIN / ORDER BY muscle memory pgvector hybrid queries lean on. Sharpen the query optimisation problems → when the interviewer probes "would the planner pick the HNSW index here?" Stack the ETL practice set → so your embedding-refresh + CDC freshness pipeline is not the first one you write under interview pressure. Layer the JOIN drills → for the pgvector + standard SQL hybrid pattern. For the broader 450+ DE-focused problem set, head to the practice home → . Lock in vector-DB muscle memory Vendor docs explain the API. PipeCode drills explain the decision — when pgvector is enough, when HNSW beats IVF, when hybrid search saves a relevance complaint. Pipecode.ai is Leetcode for Data Engineering — pattern-first practice tuned for the production trade-offs senior data engineers actually face. Practice SQL problems → Practice ETL problems →

title: Polars vs Pandas vs DuckDB Benchmarked: Speed, Memory & API Trade-offs
author: Gowtham Potureddi
summary: polars vs pandas is the single biggest dataframe-engine choice a Python data team makes in 2026 — and the one most teams get wrong on the first try because they treat all three engines as "just dataframes with a different syntax" instead of as fundamentally different execution models. Pandas is a single-threaded, Python-object-heavy library built on NumPy. Polars is a multi-threaded, Rust-core, Arrow-native engine with a lazy query optimiser. DuckDB is an in-process columnar SQL database with a cost-based planner. The methods on top look similar; the runtime reality is wildly different. This guide is the senior-DE comparison you wished existed the first time an interviewer asked "when do you pick polars vs duckdb for a 50GB join?" or "does pandas 2.0 close the gap to Polars?" It walks through the execution-model split (eager / lazy / SQL — and why polars lazy frame plans beat eager pandas by 10x on real workloads), the memory profile across the three engines ( arrow columnar vs object dtype, Polars streaming engine, DuckDB external spill), the API ergonomics trade-off ( polars dataframe expressions vs pandas index vs DuckDB SQL), and the 5-question decision tree senior data engineering python practitioners use to pick the right engine for a new pipeline. Each section pairs a teaching block with a Solution-Tail interview answer — code, a step-by-step trace, an output table, then a concept-by-concept breakdown of why it works. When you want hands-on reps immediately after reading, drill the aggregation practice library → , rehearse on optimization-flavoured problems → , and sharpen the manipulation axis with the data-manipulation drills → . On this page Why the dataframe-engine choice matters in 2026 Execution model — eager vs lazy vs SQL Memory profile — Arrow, streaming, spill API ergonomics — index vs expressions vs SQL Picking the engine — decision tree + hybrid Cheat sheet — dataframe-engine recipes Frequently asked questions Practice on PipeCode 1. Why the dataframe-engine choice matters in 2026 Pandas, Polars, and DuckDB are three execution models in one Python process — the engine choice, not the API, is what makes the engineering trade-off The one-sentence invariant: pandas runs eager and single-threaded over Python-object arrays, Polars runs a multi-threaded Rust core over an Arrow-columnar plan, and DuckDB runs a cost-based SQL planner over Arrow-columnar storage — and the right engine for a given workload is the one whose execution model matches the workload's shape . Every other comparison — speed, memory, API ergonomics, interop, hiring pool — follows from those three execution models. Once you internalise "eager Python vs lazy Rust vs SQL planner," the entire polars vs pandas vs duckdb interview surface collapses to a sequence of consequences from that one choice. Three axes interviewers actually probe. Speed. On a single-node 10GB groupby, pandas finishes in 90s, Polars in 6s, DuckDB in 4s. That is not a clever benchmark — it is the result of three execution models. Pandas walks Python objects in one thread. Polars dispatches to a Rust thread pool over Arrow buffers. DuckDB compiles a vectorised pipeline that streams Arrow blocks. 10x–20x speed swings are the default , not the outlier. Memory. Pandas materialises every intermediate result; a 10GB Parquet often becomes 30GB in RAM after read_parquet + groupby + merge . Polars's polars lazy frame plan + streaming engine can run the same workload in 4-6GB by spilling intermediates. DuckDB streams Arrow chunks through a vectorised pipeline and spills to disk when the working set exceeds memory; it can run a 100GB groupby on a laptop with 16GB of RAM. API. Pandas is the Python data lingua franca — every interviewer, every tutorial, every Stack Overflow answer uses it. Polars's expression API is cleaner but new for most candidates. DuckDB lets you write straight SQL on a dataframe, which is either the most natural API in the world (if you came from analytics) or the friction-iest (if you grew up on pandas chaining). The 2026 reality — what changed since 2022. Polars 1.x stabilised in 2024 and is now production-ready for most teams. The polars dataframe API is stable, the lazy engine ships a streaming mode for out-of-core workloads, and the Python wheels are universally installable. pandas 2.x added an optional PyArrow backend ( pd.options.future.infer_string = True and dtype_backend="pyarrow" ) that gives roughly 2-3x speedup and 2-4x memory reduction over object-dtype pandas. It does not close the gap to Polars (which is 5-10x faster on average and parallelised), but it does narrow it significantly for memory-bound workloads. DuckDB 1.x released in 2024, marking a stable on-disk format and a frozen SQL dialect for production use. The Python integration ships zero-copy con.from_df(...) for pandas and con.from_arrow(...) for Polars / PyArrow, so you can mix engines in one Python process. arrow columnar is the lingua franca of the three engines. A Polars DataFrame is an Arrow table. A DuckDB result is an Arrow table. pandas 2.x can be backed by Arrow. Zero-copy between them is free — converting Polars → DuckDB → pandas in the same process costs nothing if you stay on Arrow. The four "must-answer" axes for any engine pick. Execution model. Eager evaluation (pandas) is convenient for exploration; lazy evaluation (Polars / DuckDB) lets the engine reorder and fuse operations. Memory profile. Object-dtype pandas blows up by 3-5x compared to disk; Arrow-backed engines stay close to disk size and spill to disk when needed. API ergonomics. Indexed pandas is great at small scale and ad-hoc lookups; Polars expressions scale to long chains; DuckDB SQL is portable across teams. Ecosystem. Pandas integrates with everything (Sklearn, NumPy, Matplotlib). Polars now integrates with most things. DuckDB integrates with the SQL ecosystem (any BI tool, any ORM, any Iceberg / Parquet store). What interviewers listen for. Do you say "pandas is eager + single-thread, Polars is lazy + multi-thread, DuckDB is SQL + cost-based optimiser" in the first sentence? — senior signal. Do you describe Arrow as the shared columnar format , not just as "a faster pandas"? — required answer. Do you mention "the right answer is often hybrid — Polars for column shaping, DuckDB for joins" unprompted? — senior signal. Do you push back on "pandas 2.0 makes Polars unnecessary" with "Arrow backend is only one of three things Polars does"? — senior signal. Worked example — same groupby, three engines, three timings Detailed explanation. The classic dataframe Hello World — sum a revenue column by country from a 10GB Parquet file — looks deceptively similar in all three engines. The methods read almost identically. The runtime is wildly different: pandas walks one thread over Python objects, Polars dispatches across all cores over Arrow buffers, and DuckDB compiles a vectorised pipeline and streams Arrow chunks. Question. Compute total_revenue per country over a 10GB Parquet file in pandas (eager), Polars (lazy + collect), and DuckDB (SQL). Highlight the runtime difference (wall clock and peak RAM), and explain why the gap is structural, not implementation detail. Input. Column Type Cardinality country string ~250 customer_id int64 50M revenue float64 continuous Code. # pandas — eager, single-threaded, Python-object overhead import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) out = df . groupby ( " country " , as_index = False )[ " revenue " ]. sum () out . to_parquet ( " by_country_pandas.parquet " ) # wall: ~90s, peak RAM: ~28GB (object dtype overhead + intermediate) # Polars — lazy, multi-threaded, Arrow native import polars as pl out = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) . group_by ( " country " ) . agg ( pl . col ( " revenue " ). sum ()) . collect ( streaming = True ) ) out . write_parquet ( " by_country_polars.parquet " ) # wall: ~6s, peak RAM: ~4GB (Arrow buffers + streaming) # DuckDB — SQL, columnar, cost-based optimiser import duckdb con = duckdb . connect () con . execute ( """ COPY ( SELECT country, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM ' sales.parquet ' GROUP BY country ) TO ' by_country_duckdb.parquet ' (FORMAT PARQUET) """ ) # wall: ~4s, peak RAM: ~2.5GB (vectorised pipeline + spill) Step-by-step explanation. Pandas materialises the entire Parquet file into a DataFrame with object-dtype strings for country . Every value carries a Python-object header (~50 bytes per string) so the in-memory size is several times the on-disk size. The groupby(...).sum() call builds a Python-level hash map keyed by Python string objects. Hashing a Python string and incrementing a counter is a fast operation, but doing it 50M times in one thread takes time. Polars's scan_parquet returns a LazyFrame — a description of the work, not the data. The group_by and agg calls build a query plan. Only collect(streaming=True) triggers execution. The Polars optimiser reorders operations, prunes columns (only country and revenue are read off disk — the other columns are skipped at the Parquet level), and dispatches the work across all CPU cores. Each thread hashes a chunk of Arrow buffers. DuckDB parses the SQL, builds a logical plan, runs the cost-based optimiser ( country cardinality vs row count chooses hash-aggregate over sort-aggregate), and compiles a vectorised pipeline. Arrow blocks of ~2048 rows flow through the operators with no per-row Python overhead. The three timings — 90s, 6s, 4s — are not measurement noise. They are the result of three execution models on the same workload. The pandas → Polars gap is dominated by threading + Arrow ; the Polars → DuckDB gap is dominated by plan optimisation + vectorised pipeline fusion . Output (wall time + peak RAM benchmark). Engine Wall (10GB) Peak RAM Threads pandas (NumPy backend) 90 s 28 GB 1 pandas (Arrow backend) 45 s 12 GB 1 Polars (lazy + streaming) 6 s 4 GB all cores DuckDB 4 s 2.5 GB all cores Rule of thumb. If your workload is bigger than a few hundred MB and runs more than once, the engine choice is worth 10-20x. Pandas is for exploration and < 100MB data; Polars and DuckDB are for repeated pipelines. Worked example — eager vs lazy on a filter-then-aggregate Detailed explanation. A common pattern: read a Parquet file, filter to one country, then group by month. Pandas runs each step on the full DataFrame and only filters in step 2 — wasted work in step 1. Polars's lazy plan pushes the filter down to the Parquet scan, reading only the filtered partition. The same plan rewriting is invisible in eager engines. Question. Read a 50GB Parquet, filter country = 'US' (10% of rows), then group by month. Show the plan that pandas runs vs the plan Polars rewrites. Input. Step Operation Pandas effort Polars effort 1 Read Parquet reads all 50GB reads only country + filter cols 2 Filter country='US' scans 50GB in RAM filter pushed to Parquet predicate 3 Group by month works on 50GB DataFrame works on 5GB filtered Code. # pandas — eager, each line runs immediately import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) # loads all 50GB into RAM df_us = df [ df [ " country " ] == " US " ] # filter materialises 5GB copy out = df_us . groupby ( " month " )[ " revenue " ]. sum () # aggregate on the 5GB copy # Polars — lazy, the optimiser sees the full plan import polars as pl out = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) # no I/O yet — just a plan . filter ( pl . col ( " country " ) == " US " ) # filter in plan . group_by ( " month " ) . agg ( pl . col ( " revenue " ). sum ()) . collect ( streaming = True ) # NOW execute, with optimisations ) Step-by-step explanation. In pandas, read_parquet("sales.parquet") loads all 50GB into RAM immediately. On a 32GB laptop, the program dies before the filter ever runs. Even on a beefy machine, the filter materialises a full copy of the matched rows — 5GB more RAM — because pandas does not know the user only needs the filtered subset. Polars's scan_parquet returns a LazyFrame — a description of the work, not the data. collect(streaming=True) triggers execution. Before execution, the Polars optimiser walks the plan: filter is predicate-pushdown eligible (the filter applies to a column read directly from Parquet). The optimiser pushes the predicate down into the Parquet reader, so only rows where country='US' are ever materialised. Polars also performs projection pushdown : only country , month , and revenue are read from Parquet. The other 20 columns are skipped at the file level (Parquet's column-chunk layout makes this free). End result: Polars reads ~500MB from disk (10% of 50GB × 3 columns / 20 columns ≈ 0.75GB), uses ~2GB RAM, completes in ~5s. Pandas either OOMs or takes 4+ minutes on a 32GB machine. Output (engineering effort comparison). Aspect pandas Polars (lazy) Disk I/O 50 GB 0.75 GB (pushdown) Peak RAM OOM or 35 GB 2 GB Wall time 4+ min or fail 5 s Engineer effort rewrite to chunks one .collect() change Rule of thumb. Whenever your workload includes a filter or a column projection over a large file, lazy evaluation pays for itself instantly. The same code, with read swapped for scan , can drop runtime by 10x without any other change. Worked example — SQL over a dataframe with DuckDB Detailed explanation. A frequent interview probe: "you have a pandas DataFrame and want to run a window function on it. Can you do it without writing the pandas equivalent?" Yes — DuckDB can query a pandas DataFrame directly with zero-copy Arrow. The DataFrame becomes a virtual table in the DuckDB session and SQL runs against it. Question. Given a pandas DataFrame of orders, compute the running total of amount per customer using DuckDB SQL without copying the data. Input. order_id customer_id amount ts 1 c1 50 2026-01-01 2 c1 30 2026-01-02 3 c2 100 2026-01-01 4 c1 20 2026-01-03 Code. import duckdb import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " orders.parquet " ) con = duckdb . connect () # Register the pandas DataFrame as a virtual table — zero copy via Arrow con . register ( " orders " , df ) result = con . execute ( """ SELECT order_id, customer_id, amount, ts, SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY ts) AS running_total FROM orders ORDER BY customer_id, ts """ ). fetchdf () Step-by-step explanation. duckdb.connect() opens an in-process database. No server, no socket, no setup — it is a Python library with a SQL engine inside. con.register("orders", df) exposes the pandas DataFrame as a virtual table named orders . The DataFrame buffers are exposed as Arrow buffers (pandas 2.x can be Arrow-backed natively; older pandas gets a zero-copy conversion via PyArrow under the hood for primitive dtypes). The SQL SUM(...) OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...) is a window function. DuckDB has a mature window-function implementation — partitioning, ordering, frames, ranges, ranking, lag/lead. fetchdf() returns a pandas DataFrame again (Arrow → pandas, also zero-copy on the columnar side). The window-function result is a new DataFrame. The interop cost is zero. The pandas DataFrame is not copied to DuckDB storage; DuckDB queries it in place. The result is not copied back into pandas; it is converted with no data movement. Output. order_id customer_id amount running_total 1 c1 50 50 2 c1 30 80 4 c1 20 100 3 c2 100 100 Rule of thumb. When the SQL is more natural than the pandas / Polars chain (window functions, multi-CTE pipelines, set operations), reach for DuckDB. The zero-copy interop means it is cheaper than rewriting in pandas, not more expensive. Senior interview question on engine choice A senior interviewer often opens with: "Walk me through how you would choose between pandas, Polars, and DuckDB for a new pipeline. What are the three or four questions you ask, in order, and what answer to each one pushes you to one engine over the others?" Solution Using a 4-question decision framework Decision framework — pandas vs Polars vs DuckDB 1. How big is the data? - < 100 MB → pandas (familiar, fine for the scale) - 100 MB – 50 GB → Polars or DuckDB - > 50 GB (single node) → DuckDB (spill to disk) or Polars streaming 2. What does the pipeline look like? - column shaping, expressions → Polars (expression API) - joins + window + SQL flavour → DuckDB (cost-based optimiser) - ad-hoc exploration, plots → pandas 3. Is the workload repeated? - one-shot exploration → whatever is fastest to type - production pipeline, runs N/day → Polars or DuckDB (cost matters) 4. What does the team write fastest? - existing pandas codebase → start with pandas, hotspot to Polars / DuckDB - SQL-fluent team → DuckDB - greenfield, ok with new API → Polars Step-by-step trace. Pipeline Q1 size Q2 shape Q3 repeated Q4 team Picked Ad-hoc analyst notebook 50 MB shaping + plot one-shot pandas team pandas Daily revenue ETL 20 GB shape + groupby repeated greenfield Polars Iceberg join-heavy report 100 GB joins + window repeated SQL-fluent DuckDB ML feature build 5 GB shape + roll-window repeated greenfield Polars Quarterly dashboard 80 GB joins + agg weekly SQL-fluent DuckDB After the 4-question pass, the engine choice is usually unambiguous. The remaining 5% — where two engines both work — defaults to whatever the team already operates. Output: Engine When it wins pandas small data, ad-hoc, ecosystem-bound (Sklearn / NumPy / Matplotlib) Polars medium-to-large data, expression-heavy pipelines, repeated runs DuckDB join-heavy, SQL-first, > 5GB data, mixed Parquet / Iceberg sources Why this works — concept by concept: Execution model — the structural axis — every other consequence (speed, memory, API) follows from the engine's execution model. Asking the data-size + workload-shape question first short-circuits a lot of false dichotomies. Repeated vs one-shot — one-shot exploration rewards fastest-to-type; repeated pipelines reward fastest-to-run. The same workload with the same size can pick different engines on this question alone. Team fluency tax — engine choice has a real onboarding cost. A SQL-fluent team writes DuckDB pipelines in hours; the same team writes a Polars expression chain in days. The right engine accounts for the engineer cost, not just the wall clock. Hybrid is the senior answer — Polars for column shaping ( with_columns , expressions, complex transforms) plus DuckDB for joins and SQL (set operations, window frames, CTEs) plus Arrow as the zero-copy wire format between them — that combination beats any single engine on multi-step pipelines. Cost — engine choice is a 1-year-or-more commitment; the migration cost between engines is 1-3 months of senior-engineer time. Get the 4 questions right the first time.
