Execution model — eager vs lazy vs SQL polars vs pandas and polars vs duckdb are an execution-model story — eager Python, lazy Rust, and cost-based SQL planner respectively The mental model in one line: pandas runs each statement eagerly the moment Python evaluates it, Polars builds a polars lazy frame plan and only executes on .collect() , and DuckDB parses your SQL into a logical plan, runs a cost-based optimiser, and compiles a vectorised pipeline — and that single difference dictates which engine wins which workload . Once you say "eager Python vs lazy Rust vs SQL planner," every polars benchmark interview question becomes a deduction from those three execution models. The pandas execution model. Eager — every statement runs immediately. df.assign(x=...) allocates a new DataFrame; df["x"] = ... mutates in place. There is no plan, no deferred execution. Single-threaded — the GIL bites. Most pandas operations run in one Python thread. Some NumPy / vectorised paths drop the GIL internally, but the user-visible model is "one core." NumPy-backed by default; PyArrow-backed since 2.0. The default object -dtype path stores Python objects (slow, memory-heavy). The opt-in PyArrow backend ( dtype_backend="pyarrow" ) stores Arrow buffers — much faster, much less memory, but you have to ask for it. The mental model is "Series + Index." Every pandas operation is a Series-on-Series operation; the index aligns the two sides. Misaligned indices silently change semantics. The Polars execution model. Lazy — the plan is the program. pl.scan_parquet(...).filter(...).group_by(...) builds a LazyFrame description; .collect() triggers execution. Multi-threaded — Rust core, no GIL. Polars dispatches work across all CPU cores. The Python layer is a thin wrapper around a Rust engine that does not touch the GIL during execution. Arrow-native — zero-copy with the ecosystem. A Polars DataFrame is an Arrow table. You can hand it to DuckDB, PyArrow, or pandas 2.x without copying. The plan is optimised before execution. Predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, common-subexpression elimination, join reordering, slice pushdown — Polars applies all of them on the lazy plan. The DuckDB execution model. SQL-first — parse, plan, optimise, execute. A SQL query goes through a parser → logical plan → optimiser → physical plan → vectorised executor. Cost-based optimiser. DuckDB collects statistics about cardinalities and column distributions and uses them to reorder joins (smallest-table-first), pick the right join algorithm (hash vs merge vs nested-loop), and choose hash-aggregate vs sort-aggregate. Vectorised execution over Arrow chunks. DuckDB's runtime processes ~2048-row "chunks" through a pipeline of operators. Each operator works on whole chunks at once — no per-row Python overhead. In-process + zero-copy to dataframes. DuckDB runs inside your Python process. It can query a pandas DataFrame or a Polars DataFrame as a virtual table with no data movement. The 2026 reality — what changed since 2022. Polars 1.x stabilised the streaming engine — large queries spill intermediates to disk so the engine no longer requires the working set to fit in RAM. The streaming engine is on by default in Python 3.13+. pandas 2.x added the PyArrow backend and the dtype_backend="pyarrow" toggle. String columns went from "object Python strings" to "Arrow strings"; performance and memory both improved 2-4x. DuckDB 1.x stabilised the on-disk format (Parquet, Iceberg, native DuckDB) and introduced async I/O, parallel CSV / Parquet readers, and a much-improved out-of-core groupby with disk spill. Apache Arrow is now the lingua franca. All three engines use Arrow buffers internally; zero-copy interop is free. Common interview probes on execution model. "What is the difference between pl.scan_parquet and pl.read_parquet ?" — scan returns a LazyFrame (a plan); read returns a DataFrame (eager). "What is predicate pushdown?" — pushing a filter down into the data source so unfiltered rows are never read. "Does pandas have a query optimiser?" — no. Every operation runs eagerly; the engine cannot rearrange your code. "What is the vectorised execution model in DuckDB?" — operators process columnar chunks (~2048 rows) at a time; no per-row Python overhead. Worked example — same groupby, three execution paths Detailed explanation. A sales DataFrame with 50M rows needs total_revenue per country . The three engines reach the result via three different execution paths. Walking through each path is the cleanest way to internalise the execution-model split. Question. Compute total_revenue per country over 50M rows in pandas, Polars, and DuckDB. Trace what each engine does step by step. Input. Engine Disk format Read API Execution trigger pandas Parquet pd.read_parquet eager (immediate) Polars Parquet pl.scan_parquet .collect() DuckDB Parquet SQL FROM 'sales.parquet' con.execute(...).fetchdf() Code. # pandas — eager, every step runs immediately import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) result = df . groupby ( " country " , as_index = False )[ " revenue " ]. sum () # Polars — build a lazy plan, then collect import polars as pl plan = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) . group_by ( " country " ) . agg ( pl . col ( " revenue " ). sum ()) ) result = plan . collect () # plan is optimised + executed in parallel # DuckDB — SQL, parsed + planned + optimised + vectorised import duckdb result = duckdb . query ( """ SELECT country, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM ' sales.parquet ' GROUP BY country """ ). df () Step-by-step explanation. Pandas's read_parquet materialises the full DataFrame into RAM. The groupby builds a Python-level hash map keyed by Python strings; the result is a new DataFrame with one row per country. Polars's scan_parquet does no I/O — it returns a LazyFrame . The group_by(...).agg(...) chain extends the plan, again with no execution. .collect() runs the optimiser: projection pushdown (read only country + revenue ), predicate pushdown (none here, but the rule would apply), parallel scan (each thread reads a Parquet row group), and a parallel hash-aggregate across all cores. DuckDB parses the SQL into a logical plan, runs the cost-based optimiser (chooses hash-aggregate because country cardinality is ~250 vs row count 50M), compiles a vectorised executor, and streams Arrow chunks through it. The wall-clock gap between pandas (~90s) and Polars / DuckDB (~5s) is structural: multi-threading + Arrow + plan optimisation each contributes a multiplicative factor. Output (execution path comparison). Engine I/O cost CPU cost RAM cost pandas full file, all cols 1 thread, Python strings 28 GB (object dtype) Polars filtered cols only all cores, Arrow buffers 4 GB (streaming) DuckDB filtered cols, parallel all cores, vectorised 2.5 GB (spill if needed) Rule of thumb. Use scan_parquet (not read_parquet ) in Polars unless you actually need the full materialised frame. Use DuckDB SQL directly on 'file.parquet' strings unless you need the result in pandas first. Worked example — Polars query-plan optimisation Detailed explanation. The lazy plan is the most under-appreciated Polars feature. The optimiser handles predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, common-subexpression elimination, and slice pushdown — automatically. Looking at the plan in .explain() is the surest way to teach yourself what the engine is actually doing. Question. Walk through what the Polars optimiser does on a scan_parquet → filter → select → group_by pipeline. Show the unoptimised and optimised plans. Input. Step Operation 1 scan_parquet('sales.parquet') 2 filter(pl.col('country') == 'US') 3 select(['country', 'month', 'revenue']) 4 group_by('month').agg(pl.col('revenue').sum()) Code. import polars as pl plan = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " country " ) == " US " ) . select ([ " country " , " month " , " revenue " ]) . group_by ( " month " ) . agg ( pl . col ( " revenue " ). sum ()) ) print ( plan . explain ( optimized = False )) # raw plan print ( plan . explain ( optimized = True )) # what actually runs result = plan . collect () Step-by-step explanation. The unoptimised plan executes top-down: scan all columns of the Parquet file, filter, select 3 columns, group, aggregate. This would read every byte of the file. The optimiser walks the plan and applies projection pushdown: the final select plus the agg only use month and revenue , plus the filter needs country . So only those three columns need to be read from Parquet. Predicate pushdown: the filter country='US' is moved into the Parquet scan. Parquet's row-group statistics let the reader skip row groups where no country='US' row exists. With both pushdowns applied, the optimised plan reads ~10% of the rows × 15% of the columns ≈ 1.5% of the original file's bytes from disk. The aggregation runs in parallel across the partitions. The result is collected back to a single DataFrame. Output (plan optimisation effect). Step Unoptimised Optimised Columns read all 20 3 Rows read all 500M 50M (US only) Disk I/O 50 GB 0.75 GB Wall clock 4 min 5 s Rule of thumb. When a Polars query looks slow, run .explain(optimized=True) first. If projection or predicate pushdown is not happening, you probably wrote the query in a way that defeats the optimiser (e.g. an opaque Python UDF that the planner cannot analyse). Worked example — DuckDB cost-based join reorder Detailed explanation. A common interview question: "you join three tables — orders (10M rows), customers (1M rows), countries (250 rows) — in that order in SQL. What does DuckDB actually do?" The answer reveals the cost-based optimiser. DuckDB does not run joins in the order you wrote them; it reorders to put the smallest table on the build side. Question. Walk through what DuckDB does when given SELECT * FROM orders o JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) JOIN countries co USING(country_code) . Show the optimised plan. Input. Table Rows Key orders 10,000,000 customer_id customers 1,000,000 customer_id, country_code countries 250 country_code, country_name Code. import duckdb con = duckdb . connect () plan = con . execute ( """ EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT o.order_id, c.name, co.country_name, o.amount FROM orders o JOIN customers c USING (customer_id) JOIN countries co USING (country_code) """ ). fetchall () for row in plan : print ( row ) Step-by-step explanation. The SQL writes the joins as orders × customers × countries . A naive executor would build the join in that order — but the cost-based optimiser knows the row counts and would prefer to start from the smallest table. DuckDB collects table-row statistics (and column histograms for join keys) at plan time. It computes the join-result-size estimate for each candidate order. The optimiser reorders to countries × customers × orders for the build / probe sides: it builds a hash table on the smaller side first (countries → customers) and probes with the larger side (orders). Internally, DuckDB picks a HASH_JOIN operator for both joins (smaller side fits in memory). For the countries join, the build side is 250 rows — trivial. For the customers × orders join, the build side is 1M rows — fits comfortably. The vectorised executor streams Arrow chunks through the join pipeline; each chunk is hash-probed without per-row Python overhead. Output (plan reorder + algorithm choice). Operator Build side Probe side Algorithm HASH_JOIN (countries × customers) countries (250) customers (1M) hash on country_code HASH_JOIN (above × orders) customers (1M) orders (10M) hash on customer_id PROJECTION — — select 4 columns Rule of thumb. DuckDB's cost-based optimiser does the right thing on multi-way joins automatically. You almost never need to write join order by hand. The exception is when statistics are wrong (recent inserts, no ANALYZE ); a manual ANALYZE or a join-order hint can fix it. Senior interview question on execution model A senior interviewer might ask: "Explain why Polars's lazy execution beats pandas's eager execution by 10x on real workloads. What does the optimiser do, where does the multi-threading come from, and where would the gap shrink?" Solution Using projection pushdown + parallel scan + plan optimisation # Pandas — eager, runs each step on the full DataFrame import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) # full I/O df = df [ df [ " country " ]. isin ([ " US " , " CA " , " MX " ])] # filter after read df = df [[ " country " , " month " , " revenue " ]] # project after filter out = df . groupby ([ " country " , " month " ])[ " revenue " ]. sum () # Polars — lazy, the optimiser sees the whole plan import polars as pl plan = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " sales.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " country " ). is_in ([ " US " , " CA " , " MX " ])) . select ([ " country " , " month " , " revenue " ]) . group_by ([ " country " , " month " ]) . agg ( pl . col ( " revenue " ). sum ()) ) out = plan . collect ( streaming = True ) Step-by-step trace. Step Pandas action Polars action Saved 1 Read all 20 cols × 500M rows = 50 GB Plan filter + project, do not read yet — 2 Materialise 50 GB DataFrame Plan optimiser pushes filter + project to scan — 3 Filter (28 GB intermediate) Read 3 cols × 50M rows = 0.75 GB 49.25 GB I/O 4 Project (still 28 GB residue) (already projected) — 5 Groupby on 28 GB Parallel groupby across cores on 0.75 GB ~40x After the lazy + push-down pass, Polars reads 1.5% of the original file bytes and runs the aggregation in parallel. Pandas reads 100% of the file bytes and aggregates in one thread. Output: Engine Disk I/O Threads Wall clock Peak RAM Pandas 50 GB 1 4 min 28 GB Polars (lazy) 0.75 GB all cores 5 s 2 GB Why this works — concept by concept: Lazy plan optimisation — Polars analyses the whole expression chain before executing. Pandas executes each line the moment Python evaluates it, so there is nothing to optimise. Projection pushdown — the optimiser reads only the columns the rest of the plan needs. Parquet's column-chunk layout makes this nearly free on the storage side. Predicate pushdown — filters move down to the data source. For Parquet, that means row-group skipping via min/max statistics; for Arrow datasets, it is index-based skipping. Multi-threaded Rust core — Polars's engine is Rust, dropping the GIL completely. Every operator ( group_by , join , with_columns ) is parallelised across all CPU cores by default. Cost — the Polars optimisation is O(plan size) at planning time and pays for itself instantly on any data larger than a few hundred MB. The pandas approach is O(rows) work for every step, with no opportunity to skip work the engine already knows is unnecessary. Memory profile — Arrow, streaming, spill pandas vs duckdb on memory is a 5-10x story — arrow columnar storage, Polars streaming, and DuckDB external spill change what runs on a laptop The mental model in one line: pandas stores Python objects in row-friendly NumPy arrays (3-5x memory blow-up over disk), Polars stores Arrow columnar buffers (close to disk size, can stream out-of-core), and DuckDB streams Arrow chunks through a vectorised pipeline that spills to disk when the working set exceeds memory — and that difference is what turns "this query OOMs my laptop" into "this query runs on my laptop" . Once you say "object dtype vs Arrow vs streaming vs spill," every polars vs duckdb memory question becomes a deduction from those four memory models. The pandas memory profile. Object dtype is the killer. A column of strings stored as object is a NumPy array of Python str objects. Each str carries ~50 bytes of Python header plus the string bytes. A 1GB string column on disk often becomes 5GB in RAM. NumPy backend — copy-on-write since 2.0. Pandas 2.0 introduced pd.options.mode.copy_on_write = True , which avoids unnecessary copies on chained operations. Recommended for any 2.x deployment. PyArrow backend — opt-in. dtype_backend="pyarrow" switches string columns to Arrow strings, integer columns to Arrow integers, etc. Memory drops 2-4x; speed rises 2-3x. Intermediate materialisation. Every step of a pandas pipeline produces a full intermediate DataFrame. df.assign(x=...).filter(...).groupby(...).sum() materialises three intermediates. The Polars memory profile. Arrow buffers everywhere. Every column is an Arrow buffer — primitive types take exactly their bit width, strings are stored as a single contiguous bytes buffer + offset array. No per-element Python overhead. Streaming engine — out-of-core. Polars's streaming engine runs the query in chunks. Intermediate state lives in bounded buffers; data that exceeds memory spills to a sink and is processed in batches. No intermediates in lazy plans. The optimiser fuses adjacent operations into pipelines that pass Arrow chunks operator-to-operator without materialising the full intermediate. Categorical and dictionary encodings. Polars's categorical (and Arrow's dictionary) encoding stores low-cardinality string columns as (int, dictionary) pairs — often 10x smaller than naive strings. The DuckDB memory profile. Vectorised execution, not materialised execution. DuckDB does not materialise full intermediate tables. Operators process Arrow chunks of ~2048 rows; only the chunk and small per-operator state are in memory. External spill — disk fallback for big aggregations. When a hash-aggregate's hash table exceeds the memory budget, DuckDB spills partitions to disk and processes them in two passes. Memory budget is a knob. PRAGMA memory_limit='8GB' tells DuckDB to use at most 8GB; above that, it spills. Zero-copy with Arrow. A Polars DataFrame registered with con.register(...) shares its Arrow buffers with DuckDB — no double allocation. The 2026 reality — what changed since 2022. Pandas 2.x PyArrow backend narrowed the gap. String-heavy workloads that were 5x larger in pandas vs Polars are now 1.5-2x larger. Polars streaming engine stabilised. Out-of-core joins and group-bys that were beta in 2023 are now the default in Python 3.13+. DuckDB external spill matured. Multi-TB groupby on a 32GB laptop is now routine; the spill mechanism is transparent. Arrow IPC (Inter-Process Communication) lets you hand a DataFrame from one process to another with no serialisation — useful for multi-process pipelines. Common interview probes on memory. "Why does my pandas DataFrame use 5x its on-disk size?" — object-dtype strings; switch to PyArrow backend or categorical. "What is Polars's streaming engine?" — an out-of-core execution mode where the engine processes data in chunks and spills intermediates to disk if needed. "What does DuckDB do when a join exceeds memory?" — partitioned hash join with external spill: build side partitioned to disk, probe side iterated against each partition. "Is Arrow the same as Parquet?" — no. Arrow is an in-memory columnar format; Parquet is an on-disk columnar format. Arrow ↔ Parquet conversion is the default for reading / writing. Worked example — pandas object dtype vs Arrow backend Detailed explanation. A 5GB Parquet file with mostly string columns is loaded into pandas with the default NumPy backend, then with the PyArrow backend. The memory footprint shifts dramatically. Question. Load a 5GB Parquet (string-heavy schema) into pandas with NumPy and with PyArrow backends. Compare memory usage. Input. Column Type Cardinality user_id string (UUID) 50M event string 50 country string 250 ts timestamp continuous Code. import pandas as pd # NumPy backend (default) df_np = pd . read_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) print ( df_np . memory_usage ( deep = True ). sum () / 1e9 , " GB " ) # -> ~22 GB (each string is a Python object + bytes) # PyArrow backend df_pa = pd . read_parquet ( " events.parquet " , dtype_backend = " pyarrow " ) print ( df_pa . memory_usage ( deep = True ). sum () / 1e9 , " GB " ) # -> ~6 GB (strings stored as Arrow buffers) # Optional further compression — categorical for low-cardinality cols df_np [ " country " ] = df_np [ " country " ]. astype ( " category " ) df_np [ " event " ] = df_np [ " event " ]. astype ( " category " ) print ( df_np . memory_usage ( deep = True ). sum () / 1e9 , " GB " ) # -> ~12 GB (lower-cardinality cols dictionary-encoded) Step-by-step explanation. With the NumPy backend, each string is a Python str object. A typical UUID is 36 bytes of text + ~50 bytes of Python object header = ~86 bytes per row × 50M rows = ~4.3GB just for user_id . With the PyArrow backend, each string is stored in a single contiguous bytes buffer plus an offset array ( large_string ). For UUIDs, the bytes are exactly 36 bytes per row + offset = ~38 bytes per row × 50M = ~1.9GB. About 2.5x smaller. Categorical / dictionary encoding helps further for low-cardinality columns. event has 50 unique values; encoded as int8 + lookup , it costs ~50MB instead of ~3GB. The PyArrow backend also helps speed: arithmetic and string operations run against Arrow buffers via PyArrow's C kernels, which are 2-3x faster than NumPy's object-dtype paths. Output (memory comparison). Schema NumPy backend PyArrow backend + Categorical user_id (UUID, 50M rows) 4.3 GB 1.9 GB 1.9 GB (high-card) event (50 vals, 50M rows) 3.0 GB 0.5 GB 50 MB country (250 vals, 50M rows) 3.0 GB 0.5 GB 100 MB ts (timestamp) 0.4 GB 0.4 GB 0.4 GB Total 22 GB 6 GB 2.5 GB Rule of thumb. Use dtype_backend="pyarrow" on every pandas read_parquet call. Then use category for low-cardinality string columns. Together those two changes typically save 5-10x memory. Worked example — Polars streaming on a 50GB join Detailed explanation. A laptop with 16GB of RAM cannot hold a 50GB DataFrame in pandas. Polars's streaming engine handles it by processing the data in chunks and spilling intermediates to disk when needed. Question. Join a 50GB orders Parquet with a 2GB customers Parquet on a 16GB laptop. Show how Polars's streaming engine completes the query. Input. File Size Rows orders.parquet 50 GB 500M customers.parquet 2 GB 10M Code. import polars as pl result = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " orders.parquet " ) . join ( pl . scan_parquet ( " customers.parquet " ), on = " customer_id " , how = " inner " , ) . group_by ( " country " ) . agg ([ pl . col ( " amount " ). sum (). alias ( " total_amount " ), pl . col ( " order_id " ). count (). alias ( " num_orders " ), ]) . collect ( streaming = True ) # streaming engine — out-of-core ) result . write_parquet ( " by_country.parquet " ) Step-by-step explanation. scan_parquet on both inputs creates two LazyFrame s — no data has been read yet. The optimiser plans the join: the smaller side ( customers , 2GB) is the build side; the larger side ( orders , 50GB) is streamed through as the probe side. The streaming engine reads customers in full (fits in memory), builds the hash table in memory. It then reads orders row-group by row-group from disk (each row group is ~128MB), probes the hash table for each chunk, and emits matched rows to the next operator. The group_by("country") operator builds a partial hash-aggregate per chunk. When the aggregate's hash table exceeds the memory budget, partitions spill to disk. After all chunks are processed, the partial aggregates are merged. collect(streaming=True) returns the final aggregated result as a DataFrame. The full 50GB orders file was never materialised in RAM. Output (RAM trace during the run). Phase RAM used Notes Customers build 3 GB Arrow buffers + hash table Orders streaming 5-6 GB bounded chunk buffer + hash output Aggregate spill 6 GB peak partitions spilled to /tmp Final merge 4 GB merge spilled partitions Result write 1 GB aggregated output Rule of thumb. Use collect(streaming=True) on any Polars query where the input is larger than half your RAM. The streaming engine will spill intermediates to disk transparently; the only cost is some disk I/O. Worked example — DuckDB external spill on a 100GB groupby Detailed explanation. DuckDB's hash-aggregate operator can spill partitions to disk when the in-memory hash table exceeds the configured memory limit. This makes multi-TB aggregations possible on commodity hardware. Question. Run a 100GB groupby on a 16GB laptop with DuckDB. Show the memory budget config and what DuckDB does when the aggregate exceeds memory. Input. File Size Rows Group key cardinality events.parquet 100 GB 5B 1M (user_id) Code. import duckdb con = duckdb . connect ( " local.db " ) con . execute ( " PRAGMA memory_limit= ' 12GB '" ) # budget — spill above this con . execute ( " PRAGMA temp_directory= ' /mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp '" ) # spill dir con . execute ( """ COPY ( SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS num_events, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue, MIN(event_ts) AS first_seen, MAX(event_ts) AS last_seen FROM ' events.parquet ' GROUP BY user_id ) TO ' by_user.parquet ' (FORMAT PARQUET) """ ) Step-by-step explanation. PRAGMA memory_limit='12GB' tells DuckDB to use at most 12GB; above that, operators must spill. PRAGMA temp_directory='/mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp' directs spills to a fast SSD path. SSD vs HDD makes a 10x difference for spilled aggregates. DuckDB streams Arrow chunks (~2048 rows each) from the 100GB Parquet file through its vectorised pipeline. The hash-aggregate operator builds a partitioned hash table keyed by user_id . When the hash table exceeds 12GB, DuckDB partitions the table by hash(user_id) % N and spills the cold partitions to /mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp . Only the hot partitions stay in memory. After scanning the input, DuckDB merges spilled partitions back in (reading them in batches), finalises the aggregates, and writes the result to by_user.parquet . The whole run completes in ~15 minutes on a 16GB laptop. Peak RAM stays under 12GB; peak disk usage in /mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp is ~30GB. Output (resource use). Metric Value Input size 100 GB Peak RAM 12 GB (capped by memory_limit) Peak spill disk 30 GB Output size 250 MB Wall clock ~15 min Rule of thumb. Always set PRAGMA memory_limit to ~75% of available RAM when running DuckDB on big workloads. Always set temp_directory to a fast SSD path. Without these, DuckDB defaults can either OOM or spill to a slow disk. Senior interview question on memory profile A senior interviewer might ask: "A pandas pipeline OOMs on a 32GB machine processing a 10GB Parquet file. Walk me through the diagnosis and the three fixes you would consider, in order of effort." Solution Using Arrow backend + categorical + chunked streaming # Diagnosis — pandas object dtype + intermediate materialisation import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) # 22 GB after read df = df [ df [ " country " ]. isin ([ " US " , " CA " , " MX " ])] # +8 GB intermediate df = df . merge ( users , on = " user_id " ) # OOM here # Fix 1 — switch to Arrow backend (lowest effort) df = pd . read_parquet ( " events.parquet " , dtype_backend = " pyarrow " ) # 6 GB df [ " country " ] = df [ " country " ]. astype ( " string[pyarrow] " ) df [ " event " ] = df [ " event " ]. astype ( " category " ) # now fits at ~3 GB; the rest of the pipeline runs # Fix 2 — switch to Polars (medium effort) import polars as pl out = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " country " ). is_in ([ " US " , " CA " , " MX " ])) . join ( pl . scan_parquet ( " users.parquet " ), on = " user_id " ) . collect ( streaming = True ) ) # Fix 3 — switch to DuckDB (depends on team SQL fluency) import duckdb out = duckdb . query ( """ SELECT * FROM ' events.parquet ' e JOIN ' users.parquet ' u USING (user_id) WHERE e.country IN ( ' US ' , ' CA ' , ' MX ' ) """ ). pl () # return as Polars / pandas Step-by-step trace. Step Diagnosis (pandas NumPy) Fix 1 (pandas + Arrow) Fix 2 (Polars streaming) Read 22 GB 6 GB scan only — 0 GB Filter +8 GB intermediate +2 GB intermediate predicate pushdown — 0 GB Join OOM completes at ~12 GB completes at ~5 GB Wall clock (fails) 70 s 14 s After the fix sequence, the pipeline runs reliably on the same machine. Fix 1 is one line of code; Fix 2 is a rewrite but pays back 5x in wall clock; Fix 3 is the right answer if the team is SQL-fluent and the pipeline is permanent. Output: Fix Effort Peak RAM Wall clock Pandas + Arrow backend one config line 12 GB 70 s Polars streaming rewrite as scan_* 5 GB 14 s DuckDB SQL rewrite as SQL 3 GB 10 s Why this works — concept by concept: Object dtype is the killer — Python string objects carry ~50 bytes of header each. Switching to Arrow strings cuts that to the actual bytes plus an offset table; 3-5x memory savings. Categorical for low cardinality — columns with < ~1000 unique values are best stored as (int8, dictionary) . Pandas, Polars, and Arrow all support this; the savings are 10x+ on string-heavy low-cardinality columns. Predicate pushdown beats post-read filtering — once you switch to Polars's scan_parquet , the filter moves into the Parquet reader. The unfiltered rows are never materialised. Streaming engine bounds working set — Polars's streaming engine and DuckDB's external spill both bound the working set to a configured memory budget. The system never crosses that line; intermediates spill to disk. Cost — Fix 1 is O(1) engineering effort and 4x memory savings. Fix 2 is O(rewrite) but 7x memory savings + 5x wall clock. Pick the fix the team can sustain. Python Topic — data structures Columnar memory & data-structure problems Practice → Python Topic — optimization Out-of-core & streaming optimization Practice → 4. API ergonomics — index vs expressions vs SQL polars dataframe expressions, pandas index semantics, and DuckDB SQL are three different ways to write the same query — pick the one your team writes fastest The mental model in one line: pandas's API is built around a Series + Index abstraction (every column is a Series, every operation aligns on the index), Polars's API is built around expressions on columns (every operation is a pl.col(...).<expr> over the whole frame), and DuckDB's API is SQL (parsed, planned, executed) — and the right API for a team is the one its engineers write fastest under pressure . Once you say "index vs expression vs SQL," every polars dataframe API question becomes a comparison of those three mental models. Pandas — Series + Index. Every column is a Series . A Series is a 1D labelled array with an Index. Operations between two Series align on the Index — that is the pandas idiom. The Index is a first-class object. Reset, set, multi-index, hierarchical — the index is what makes pandas powerful for lookups and what makes it confusing for newcomers. Inplace vs copy is its own world. df["x"] = ... mutates; df.assign(x=...) returns a new DataFrame; df.loc[..., "x"] = ... mutates with index alignment. The 2.x copy_on_write mode standardises this — copy semantics by default, explicit mutation when needed. Method chaining is possible but fragile. Pipelines are usually broken across df1 = ... , df2 = df1.assign(...) lines because chaining preserves intermediate state. Polars — expressions + chains. Every operation is a pl.col(...).<expr> . Expressions are first-class; you can save them, reuse them, build them programmatically. with_columns adds columns; select projects; group_by + agg aggregates. Three primary verbs, plus filter , join , sort , pivot . No index. Polars deliberately omits the pandas Index. Joining is always explicit ( how="inner" , on="key" ). Aligning two columns is always explicit. Method chaining is the natural idiom. A whole pipeline reads top-to-bottom as one . chain. Easy to read once you train your eye. DuckDB — SQL. The query is a SQL string. Subqueries, CTEs, window functions, set operations — all standard SQL. Relational API exists too. con.table("orders").join(...).aggregate(...) is a fluent API that maps to SQL internally. Most users stay on raw SQL. Result types. con.execute(...).df() returns a pandas DataFrame; .pl() returns a Polars DataFrame; .arrow() returns a PyArrow Table; .fetchall() returns Python tuples. SQL is portable. A DuckDB SQL query is mostly the same query you would run on Snowflake / BigQuery / Postgres. The translation cost is small. Interop — Arrow ties them together. Pandas → Polars. pl.from_pandas(df) — zero-copy for Arrow-backed pandas; otherwise a fast conversion. Polars → pandas. df.to_pandas(use_pyarrow_extension_array=True) — zero-copy with the PyArrow extension array. Polars → DuckDB. con.register("name", polars_df) — zero-copy; the DataFrame is a virtual table. DuckDB → Polars. con.execute(...).pl() — converts the result to a Polars DataFrame. Pandas → DuckDB. con.register("name", pandas_df) — zero-copy for Arrow-backed pandas; otherwise PyArrow converts under the hood. Common interview probes on API. "What is the difference between df.assign and df["x"] = ... in pandas?" — assign returns a new DataFrame; df["x"] = ... mutates in place. "What is an expression in Polars?" — a first-class object representing a computation on a column; can be saved, reused, composed. "Why does Polars not have an index?" — Polars treats the index as an implicit alignment that creates bugs in pandas. Explicit joins are clearer. "How do I use SQL on a Polars DataFrame?" — duckdb.query("SELECT ... FROM polars_df") works directly; polars_df.sql("...") also exists. Worked example — same window function, three APIs Detailed explanation. A window function — rank rows within a group by some ordering — is the classic test of API ergonomics. Pandas, Polars, and DuckDB all express it, each with a different idiom. Question. Compute the rank of revenue within each country for an orders DataFrame. Write the same query in all three engines. Input. order_id country revenue 1 US 100 2 US 50 3 US 200 4 CA 80 5 CA 30 Code. # Pandas — assign + groupby + rank import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " orders.parquet " ) df [ " rank_in_country " ] = ( df . groupby ( " country " )[ " revenue " ] . rank ( method = " dense " , ascending = False ) ) # Polars — with_columns + over import polars as pl df = pl . read_parquet ( " orders.parquet " ) df = df . with_columns ( pl . col ( " revenue " ) . rank ( method = " dense " , descending = True ) . over ( " country " ) . alias ( " rank_in_country " ) ) # DuckDB — SQL import duckdb df = duckdb . query ( """ SELECT *, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS rank_in_country FROM ' orders.parquet ' """ ). df () Step-by-step explanation. Pandas: df.groupby("country")["revenue"].rank(...) returns a new Series aligned with the original DataFrame's index. Assigning it to df["rank_in_country"] adds the column. Index alignment matters — if the input DataFrame had a non-default index, alignment would carry through. Polars: pl.col("revenue").rank(...) is an expression on the revenue column. .over("country") says "compute this expression per group of country ." .with_columns(...) adds the result as a new column. DuckDB: standard SQL — DENSE_RANK() is a window function with PARTITION BY (the group) and ORDER BY (the ranking). The three results are identical. The mental load differs: pandas requires the user to know how groupby + rank aligns; Polars requires the user to know the over clause; DuckDB requires the user to know SQL window functions. Output. order_id country revenue rank_in_country 3 US 200 1 1 US 100 2 2 US 50 3 4 CA 80 1 5 CA 30 2 Rule of thumb. When the query is a window function, all three engines are roughly equal in ergonomics. Pick whichever the team writes fastest — typically the existing one. Worked example — complex chain with conditional logic Detailed explanation. A non-trivial pipeline — filter, derive several columns including conditional logic, group, aggregate, sort — showcases each engine's API style. Question. From an orders table, filter to 2026 orders, derive is_large (>100), derive quarter , group by country + quarter , count orders + sum large-order revenue, sort by total revenue desc. Write in pandas, Polars, and DuckDB. Input. order_id order_ts country revenue 1 2026-01-15 US 50 2 2026-02-20 US 200 3 2026-04-05 CA 150 Code. # Pandas import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " orders.parquet " ) df = df [ df [ " order_ts " ]. dt . year == 2026 ] df [ " is_large " ] = df [ " revenue " ] > 100 df [ " quarter " ] = df [ " order_ts " ]. dt . quarter out = ( df . groupby ([ " country " , " quarter " ]) . agg ( num_orders = ( " order_id " , " count " ), large_revenue = ( " revenue " , lambda s : s [ df . loc [ s . index , " is_large " ]]. sum ()), ) . reset_index () . sort_values ( " large_revenue " , ascending = False ) ) # Polars import polars as pl out = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " orders.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " order_ts " ). dt . year () == 2026 ) . with_columns ([ ( pl . col ( " revenue " ) > 100 ). alias ( " is_large " ), pl . col ( " order_ts " ). dt . quarter (). alias ( " quarter " ), ]) . group_by ([ " country " , " quarter " ]) . agg ([ pl . col ( " order_id " ). count (). alias ( " num_orders " ), pl . col ( " revenue " ). filter ( pl . col ( " is_large " )). sum (). alias ( " large_revenue " ), ]) . sort ( " large_revenue " , descending = True ) . collect () ) # DuckDB import duckdb out = duckdb . query ( """ SELECT country, EXTRACT(quarter FROM order_ts) AS quarter, COUNT(*) AS num_orders, SUM(revenue) FILTER (WHERE revenue > 100) AS large_revenue FROM ' orders.parquet ' WHERE EXTRACT(year FROM order_ts) = 2026 GROUP BY country, quarter ORDER BY large_revenue DESC """ ). pl () Step-by-step explanation. Pandas: each step is a separate statement. The conditional large_revenue aggregation requires a lambda that re-aligns the boolean mask — clunky. Polars: pl.col("revenue").filter(pl.col("is_large")).sum() is a single expression that filters inside the aggregate. No lambda, no re-alignment. DuckDB: SUM(revenue) FILTER (WHERE ...) is standard SQL — the filtered aggregate is a built-in. The Polars and DuckDB versions are arguably more readable than the pandas version because the conditional aggregate has a first-class syntax in both. Wall-clock on 10M rows: pandas ~45s, Polars ~3s, DuckDB ~2.5s. Output. country quarter num_orders large_revenue US 2 1,200,000 240,000,000 CA 1 800,000 160,000,000 US 1 1,000,000 150,000,000 Rule of thumb. When the pipeline includes conditional aggregations, window functions, or multi-step CTEs, Polars and DuckDB both have cleaner syntax than pandas. The pandas idiom for these is often a lambda or a multi-step assignment. Worked example — interop between engines Detailed explanation. A senior engineer often mixes engines in one Python process: Polars for column shaping, DuckDB for joins, pandas for the final delivery format (because some downstream tool expects pandas). Arrow is the glue. Question. Build a hybrid pipeline: read with Polars (lazy), join with DuckDB (cost-based), return to pandas for a Sklearn step. Show the interop calls. Input. Step Engine Reason Read + shape Polars scan + filter + with_columns Join DuckDB cost-based optimiser on 3-way join Sklearn fit pandas Sklearn expects pandas / numpy Code. import polars as pl import duckdb import pandas as pd # 1. Polars — read + filter + shape events = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " ts " ). dt . year () == 2026 ) . with_columns ( pl . col ( " amount " ). log (). alias ( " log_amount " )) . collect () # Polars DataFrame ) users = pl . scan_parquet ( " users.parquet " ). collect () # Polars DataFrame # 2. DuckDB — register both Polars DFs as virtual tables, join via SQL con = duckdb . connect () con . register ( " events " , events ) con . register ( " users " , users ) joined = con . execute ( """ SELECT e.*, u.country, u.cohort FROM events e JOIN users u USING (user_id) """ ). pl () # back as Polars DataFrame # 3. Pandas — for the Sklearn step df = joined . to_pandas ( use_pyarrow_extension_array = True ) from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor X = df [[ " log_amount " , " cohort " ]] y = df [ " target " ] model = GradientBoostingRegressor (). fit ( X , y ) Step-by-step explanation. pl.scan_parquet(...).collect() materialises a Polars DataFrame. The Arrow buffers underneath are the in-memory format. con.register("events", events) exposes the Polars DataFrame's Arrow buffers as a DuckDB virtual table. Zero copy — DuckDB queries the buffers in place. The SQL join runs in DuckDB's cost-based optimiser. The result is converted back to a Polars DataFrame with .pl() , again zero-copy (Arrow → Arrow). to_pandas(use_pyarrow_extension_array=True) converts the Polars DataFrame to pandas, keeping the Arrow buffers under PyArrow extension arrays. The pandas API now sits on top of the same buffers. Sklearn's .fit(X, y) accepts pandas DataFrames natively; it converts to NumPy under the hood, which is fast for primitive dtypes. Output (interop cost analysis). Step Format change Memory cost Polars → DuckDB Arrow → Arrow 0 (shared buffers) DuckDB → Polars Arrow → Arrow 0 (shared buffers) Polars → pandas Arrow → Arrow (PyArrow extension) 0 (shared buffers) pandas → Sklearn (NumPy) conversion to NumPy on dense data small per-column copy Rule of thumb. Within a Python process, all conversion between Polars / DuckDB / pandas (Arrow-backed) is zero-copy. The only conversion that costs real memory is to NumPy / Sklearn / Matplotlib — and even that is bounded to the columns Sklearn actually reads. Senior interview question on API trade-offs A senior interviewer might ask: "You inherit a team using pandas for everything. The pipelines are slow and frequently OOM. How do you migrate to Polars without rewriting everything overnight?" Solution Using progressive migration + Arrow interop # Phase 1 — switch reads to pandas Arrow backend (one config line per read) import pandas as pd df = pd . read_parquet ( " events.parquet " , dtype_backend = " pyarrow " ) # 3-5x memory savings; team workflow unchanged # Phase 2 — hotspot rewrite (rewrite the slowest 3-5 pipelines to Polars) import polars as pl out = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " country " ) == " US " ) . group_by ( " month " ). agg ( pl . col ( " revenue " ). sum ()) . collect ( streaming = True ) ) df = out . to_pandas ( use_pyarrow_extension_array = True ) # 5-10x wall clock on hotspot pipelines; the rest stays pandas # Phase 3 — DuckDB for join-heavy reports (rewrite as SQL when SQL is clearer) import duckdb con = duckdb . connect () result = con . execute ( """ SELECT u.country, SUM(o.amount) AS total FROM ' orders.parquet ' o JOIN ' users.parquet ' u USING (user_id) WHERE o.ts >= ' 2026-01-01 ' GROUP BY u.country """ ). df () # Join + aggregate is the DuckDB sweet spot # Phase 4 — Greenfield Polars / DuckDB only; legacy pandas kept for ecosystem (Sklearn, plots) Step-by-step trace. Phase Action Effort Payoff 1 Add dtype_backend="pyarrow" everywhere 30 min total 3-5x RAM savings 2 Rewrite hottest 3-5 pipelines to Polars 2-3 weeks 5-10x speedup on those 3 Rewrite join-heavy reports as DuckDB SQL 1 week 5-10x on joins, cleaner SQL 4 Greenfield → Polars / DuckDB; legacy → pandas ongoing gradual migration After the four phases, the team has moved 70-80% of pipelines off pandas — without a "rewrite everything" sprint. Pandas stays for Sklearn / Matplotlib glue, where its ecosystem still dominates. Output: Phase Wall-clock delta RAM delta Team friction 1 (Arrow) 1.5x 3-5x lower zero 2 (Polars hotspots) 5-10x 5-7x lower medium (new API) 3 (DuckDB joins) 5-10x 4-6x lower low (SQL familiar) 4 (greenfield) applies to new work applies to new work low Why this works — concept by concept: Progressive migration — rewriting everything at once is a recipe for failure. Phase 1 (Arrow backend) is one config line; Phase 2 (hotspot Polars) is a few-week project; Phases 3-4 happen organically. Arrow interop is the bridge — because pandas (Arrow), Polars, and DuckDB all share Arrow buffers, a hybrid codebase is cheaper than a homogeneous one. Conversions are zero-copy. Hotspot rewrite delivers most of the value — the 80/20 rule applies. Five pipelines doing 80% of compute, rewritten to Polars or DuckDB, deliver the bulk of the wall-clock and RAM savings. SQL where SQL is clearer — DuckDB lets SQL-fluent engineers contribute without learning the Polars expression API. The result is a heterogeneous codebase optimised for the team's existing fluency. Cost — Phase 1 is one engineer-day for any codebase. Phase 2 is engineer-weeks per pipeline. Phase 3-4 amortises over years. The migration pays for itself in cloud bill within 6-12 months on most data teams. Picking the engine — decision tree + hybrid Senior engineers pick polars vs pandas vs duckdb from a 5-question decision tree — never from "which is faster" The mental model in one line: the engine choice is a workload-shape decision (data size, pipeline shape, repeat frequency, team fluency, sink format), driven by 5 questions — not by raw benchmark numbers and not by which engine is newest . Once you internalise the decision tree, the polars benchmark interview surface collapses to "what does your pipeline actually need?" The senior answer is almost always hybrid — Polars for shaping, DuckDB for joins, pandas for ecosystem integration. The 5 questions in order. Q1 — Data size. < 100MB → pandas works fine. 100MB - 50GB → Polars or DuckDB. > 50GB on a single node → DuckDB (spill) or Polars streaming. Q2 — Pipeline shape. Column shaping + expressions → Polars. Joins + window functions + SQL flavour → DuckDB. Ad-hoc exploration + plots → pandas. Q3 — Repeat frequency. One-shot exploration → fastest-to-type wins. Daily pipeline / production → fastest-to-run wins. Q4 — Team fluency. Existing pandas team → start with pandas, rewrite hotspots. SQL-fluent team → DuckDB. Greenfield → Polars or DuckDB. Q5 — Sink + ecosystem. Writing to Parquet / Iceberg / Delta → any of them. Sklearn / Matplotlib / SciPy → pandas (ecosystem). BI tools / SQL clients → DuckDB. Scale envelope cheat sheet. Pandas — sweet spot. < 100MB data, ad-hoc analysis, ecosystem-heavy (Sklearn / Matplotlib / SciPy / Jupyter). Above 1GB on a 16GB machine, memory and speed both fall apart unless you switch to the Arrow backend. Polars — sweet spot. 100MB - 50GB data, repeated pipelines, expression-heavy transforms, multi-core machines. Above 50GB, the streaming engine handles it but spill / disk I/O dominates wall clock. DuckDB — sweet spot. Join-heavy workloads, SQL-first teams, > 5GB data, mixed Parquet / Iceberg / CSV sources. The cost-based optimiser shines on multi-table queries. The hybrid pattern — the senior answer. Polars for column shaping. Use Polars's expression API for derive / transform / filter / aggregate. The expression API beats SQL for column-by-column derivations. DuckDB for joins. Use DuckDB SQL for joins and window functions. The cost-based optimiser picks the right join order automatically. Pandas for ecosystem. Convert to pandas only at the edges where Sklearn / Matplotlib / Statsmodels / SciPy demands a pandas DataFrame. Arrow as the wire. All three engines share Arrow buffers; interop is zero-copy. Ops cost — the hidden line item. Pandas ops. Nothing — it is a Python library. The cost is in CPU and RAM at runtime. Polars ops. Nothing extra — it is a Python library too. Streaming mode requires fast local disk for spills. DuckDB ops. Nothing extra — in-process database. Out-of-core queries need a configured temp_directory on fast SSD. All three — pip-installable, no servers, no clusters. The whole comparison is about workload fit, not ops complexity. Hiring pool. Pandas — every Python data engineer knows pandas. Largest pool, lowest hiring cost. Polars — growing fast; most modern data teams in 2026 have at least one Polars-fluent engineer. DuckDB — SQL-fluent engineers can pick it up in days; the SQL dialect is mostly Postgres-compatible. Hiring is easy via the SQL pool. Common interview probes on engine selection. "When is pandas the right choice?" — small data, ad-hoc exploration, ecosystem integration (Sklearn / Matplotlib). "When is Polars overkill?" — < 100MB data, one-shot analysis, no repeat use. The setup cost (learning expressions) outweighs the wall-clock gain. "When is DuckDB overkill?" — pure column-shaping workloads with no joins. Polars expressions are cleaner. "Why not Dask / Modin / Vaex?" — they exist but have smaller communities and fewer production deployments in 2026. The Polars + DuckDB pair covers 95% of the workloads they targeted. Worked example — Q1 size forces DuckDB on a 100GB join Detailed explanation. A 100GB Parquet file with a join is the canonical "pandas falls over" workload. On a 16GB laptop, pandas OOMs; Polars streaming works but takes 20+ minutes; DuckDB finishes in under 10 minutes thanks to its cost-based optimiser and external spill. Question. Walk through Q1 (data size) for a 100GB Parquet + 5GB join workload. Show why DuckDB is the right answer. Input. File Size Rows events.parquet 100 GB 5B users.parquet 5 GB 50M Code. import duckdb con = duckdb . connect () con . execute ( " PRAGMA memory_limit= ' 12GB '" ) con . execute ( " PRAGMA temp_directory= ' /mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp '" ) result = con . execute ( """ SELECT u.country, COUNT(*) AS n, SUM(e.revenue) AS total FROM ' events.parquet ' e JOIN ' users.parquet ' u USING (user_id) GROUP BY u.country """ ). df () Step-by-step explanation. Pandas cannot handle 100GB on a 16GB machine; the program OOMs on read_parquet . Polars's streaming engine can do it, but the streaming join on a 100GB × 5GB pair is heavy on disk I/O. Wall clock ~25 minutes on the laptop. DuckDB's cost-based optimiser picks the smaller side ( users , 5GB) as build and the larger side ( events , 100GB) as probe. The build-side hash table fits in memory (5GB < 12GB limit). The probe side streams chunk-by-chunk against the hash table; no spill needed for the join. The GROUP BY country aggregate has low cardinality (~250 countries), so its hash table is tiny. The whole query completes in ~8 minutes on the laptop. Output (engine comparison for the workload). Engine Wall clock Peak RAM Notes Pandas OOM 32 GB Cannot fit Polars (streaming) ~25 min 10 GB Heavy disk I/O DuckDB ~8 min 12 GB Optimal join order Rule of thumb. For > 50GB workloads with joins, DuckDB is the default. Its cost-based optimiser plus external spill plus vectorised execution make it the most efficient single-node engine for that scale. Worked example — Q2 shape forces Polars on a long expression chain Detailed explanation. A workload that derives 15 new columns from existing columns via complex expressions is the canonical "Polars expressions win" workload. SQL gets verbose; pandas gets slow; Polars expressions stay concise. Question. Derive 15 features from an events DataFrame — logs, lags, ratios, rolling means, conditional flags — and write the result. Show why Polars is the right choice. Input. Feature Type log_revenue log of revenue revenue_lag_1 lag 1 row revenue_to_max ratio to per-user max revenue_z z-score per user is_outlier revenue > 3σ ...10 more various Code. import polars as pl out = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) . with_columns ([ pl . col ( " revenue " ). log (). alias ( " log_revenue " ), pl . col ( " revenue " ). shift ( 1 ). over ( " user_id " ). alias ( " revenue_lag_1 " ), ( pl . col ( " revenue " ) / pl . col ( " revenue " ). max (). over ( " user_id " )) . alias ( " revenue_to_max " ), (( pl . col ( " revenue " ) - pl . col ( " revenue " ). mean (). over ( " user_id " )) / pl . col ( " revenue " ). std (). over ( " user_id " )). alias ( " revenue_z " ), ( pl . col ( " revenue " ) > pl . col ( " revenue " ). mean (). over ( " user_id " ) + 3 * pl . col ( " revenue " ). std (). over ( " user_id " )). alias ( " is_outlier " ), pl . col ( " revenue " ). rolling_mean ( 7 ). over ( " user_id " ). alias ( " revenue_rmean7 " ), pl . col ( " amount " ). cum_sum (). over ( " user_id " ). alias ( " cum_amount " ), # ... ]) . collect ( streaming = True ) ) Step-by-step explanation. Each pl.col(...).<expr>.over("user_id") is an expression that computes a per-user value. The expressions are first-class objects, composable, and the optimiser can fuse them. The with_columns([...]) call adds all 15 derived columns in one go. The optimiser walks the list, identifies shared sub-expressions ( mean().over("user_id") appears in revenue_z and is_outlier ), and computes them once. The lazy plan executes in parallel across all CPU cores. Each partition computes its per-user windowed expressions; results are concatenated. The equivalent in pandas requires 15 separate df.assign(...) calls or a complex groupby.apply block that runs in Python — orders of magnitude slower. The equivalent in DuckDB is a long SQL with 15 window functions in the SELECT clause — works but is verbose and harder to read than the Polars expression list. Output (engine comparison for the workload). Engine Code size Wall clock Readability Pandas ~30 lines 4 min (10M rows) medium Polars ~15 lines 25 s high (composable) DuckDB ~60 lines SQL 30 s medium (long SELECT) Rule of thumb. When the workload is column-shaping-heavy with shared sub-expressions and per-group windows, Polars's expression API wins on both wall clock and readability. Worked example — hybrid pattern for a production pipeline Detailed explanation. A real production pipeline rarely picks one engine. The senior pattern: Polars for the heavy column-shaping, DuckDB for the joins, pandas for the Sklearn / Matplotlib edges. Arrow ties them together. Question. Build a daily feature pipeline: read 30GB of raw events, derive 20 features per user, join with a slowly-changing dimension table, feed the result to a Sklearn model. Show the hybrid architecture. Input. Step Engine Reason Read + shape Polars scan + filter + with_columns Join with users DuckDB cost-based join optimiser Sklearn fit pandas ecosystem requirement Code. import polars as pl import duckdb import pandas as pd from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor # 1) Polars — feature derivation features = ( pl . scan_parquet ( " events.parquet " ) . filter ( pl . col ( " ts " ). dt . year () == 2026 ) . with_columns ([ pl . col ( " revenue " ). log (). alias ( " log_revenue " ), pl . col ( " revenue " ). mean (). over ( " user_id " ). alias ( " user_avg_rev " ), pl . col ( " revenue " ). std (). over ( " user_id " ). alias ( " user_std_rev " ), # ...17 more features ]) . collect ( streaming = True ) ) # 2) DuckDB — join with the user dimension table con = duckdb . connect () con . register ( " features " , features ) con . execute ( " SET temp_directory= ' /mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp '" ) joined = con . execute ( """ SELECT f.*, u.country, u.cohort, u.tier FROM features f JOIN ' users.parquet ' u USING (user_id) """ ). pl () # 3) Pandas — for Sklearn df = joined . to_pandas ( use_pyarrow_extension_array = True ) X = df [[ " log_revenue " , " user_avg_rev " , " user_std_rev " , " tier " ]] y = df [ " target " ] model = GradientBoostingRegressor (). fit ( X , y ) Step-by-step explanation. Polars handles the feature derivation. The lazy plan + projection pushdown means only the columns used downstream are read from Parquet. The Polars DataFrame is registered with DuckDB via con.register(...) — zero-copy, the Arrow buffers are shared. DuckDB's optimiser picks the build / probe side for the join with the users.parquet table. The result returns to Polars via .pl() — again zero-copy. Conversion to pandas is the last step, and only because Sklearn expects pandas / NumPy. The conversion is zero-copy at the Arrow level; pandas wraps the Arrow buffers. The whole pipeline runs in a single Python process, with three engines, no serialisation overhead between them. Output (per-engine contribution). Stage Engine Wall clock Why this engine Read + shape Polars 35 s expressions + lazy + multi-core Join DuckDB 12 s cost-based optimiser Sklearn fit pandas (Sklearn) 90 s ecosystem requirement Total hybrid 137 s best of three Rule of thumb. Hybrid pipelines beat single-engine pipelines on most multi-step workloads. The 2026 senior answer is "use the right engine for each step; let Arrow handle interop." Senior interview question on engine selection in 2026 A senior interviewer might frame this as: "You join a team that runs all their data pipelines in pandas. The pipelines are slow and OOM frequently. Walk me through how you'd pick between pandas, Polars, and DuckDB for each pipeline, and what migration plan you'd propose." Solution Using the 5-question framework + progressive migration Engine selection — pandas vs Polars vs DuckDB ============================================= Q1 Data size → <100MB? pandas. <50GB? Polars or DuckDB. >50GB? DuckDB or Polars streaming. Q2 Pipeline shape → expressions-heavy? Polars. Joins + SQL? DuckDB. Ad-hoc? pandas. Q3 Repeat freq → one-shot? fastest-to-type. Daily/prod? fastest-to-run. Q4 Team fluency → existing pandas? migrate hotspots. SQL-fluent? DuckDB. Greenfield? Polars. Q5 Sink/ecosystem → Sklearn/MPL? pandas at the edge. Iceberg/Parquet/BI? DuckDB or Polars. Engine-specific trade-offs -------------------------- Pandas + Huge ecosystem (Sklearn, MPL, SciPy) + Easiest API for newcomers + Smallest setup cost − Eager only, single-threaded − Object-dtype memory blow-up − Crashes above ~1-5GB on a 16GB box Polars + Lazy + multi-threaded + Arrow native + Streaming engine for out-of-core + Cleanest expression API − Smaller ecosystem than pandas − Less familiar to a pandas team − Steeper learning curve for non-functional programmers DuckDB + Cost-based SQL optimiser + External spill for huge aggregates / joins + SQL portable across cloud DWHs − SQL only (or relational API, less idiomatic) − Window-function quirks vs Postgres − One Python connection per DuckDB instance Step-by-step trace. Existing pipeline Q1 size Q2 shape Q3 freq Q4 team Q5 sink Pick Ad-hoc analyst notebook 50 MB shape+plot one-shot pandas MPL pandas Daily revenue ETL 20 GB shape+groupby daily pandas Parquet Polars Iceberg join report 100 GB joins+window weekly SQL-fluent Iceberg DuckDB ML feature build 5 GB shape+window daily pandas Sklearn Polars + pandas hybrid BI export 80 GB joins+agg daily SQL-fluent BI tool DuckDB Five Q-pass results → three engines used; greenfield work goes Polars / DuckDB; legacy ad-hoc stays pandas; ML pipelines use hybrid. Output: Verdict Reasoning Migrate revenue ETL to Polars 20GB streaming + expression-heavy + daily run Migrate Iceberg report to DuckDB join-heavy + SQL-fluent team + large data Hybrid for ML features Polars shape + DuckDB join + pandas for Sklearn Keep ad-hoc in pandas small data + ecosystem requirement Migrate BI export to DuckDB SQL + BI tool + join + scale Why this works — concept by concept: 5 questions in order — answering Q1 (size) first eliminates options early. If Q1 forces DuckDB (>50GB), you don't waste time evaluating pandas. Hybrid is normal — most production pipelines use 2 or 3 engines. Arrow's zero-copy interop makes hybrid cheaper than homogeneous in many cases. Progressive migration — never rewrite everything. Switch reads to Arrow backend first (one config line); rewrite the slowest 3-5 pipelines to Polars / DuckDB; leave the rest. The 80/20 rule applies. Team fluency tax — engine choice has a real onboarding cost. A pandas-only team migrating to Polars needs 2-4 weeks of ramp. A SQL-fluent team picks up DuckDB in days. Cost — engine choice is a multi-year commitment for production pipelines. Migration cost is 1-3 months of senior-engineer time per major pipeline. Get the 5 questions right the first time. Python Topic — ETL ETL engine trade-off problems Practice → Python Topic — data manipulation Hybrid dataframe-engine pipelines Practice → Cheat sheet — dataframe-engine recipes When pandas is enough. < 100MB data, ad-hoc exploration, Sklearn / Matplotlib integration. The default for analyst-led notebooks; the wrong default for production pipelines above 1GB. When you need Polars. 100MB - 50GB data, expression-heavy transforms (per-group windows, complex derived columns), repeated pipelines, multi-core machines, want lazy evaluation + multi-threading for free. When you need DuckDB. > 5GB data, join-heavy, window-function-heavy, SQL-first team, mixed Parquet / Iceberg / CSV sources, BI-tool integration. The cost-based optimiser is the killer feature. The hybrid recipe. Polars for column shaping ( with_columns , expressions, per-group windows) + DuckDB for joins and window-heavy SQL + pandas only at the edges for Sklearn / Matplotlib / Statsmodels. Arrow is the zero-copy wire between them. Pandas 2.x Arrow backend. Set dtype_backend="pyarrow" on every read_parquet . Use pd.options.mode.copy_on_write = True globally. Convert low-cardinality string columns to category . Together: 3-5x memory savings, 2-3x speedup, zero rewrite cost. Polars LazyFrame template. pl.scan_parquet(...).filter(...).with_columns([...]).group_by(...).agg([...]).collect(streaming=True) — 6 lines for a typical pipeline. Always scan_* , never read_* for large data; always collect(streaming=True) if input > half RAM. DuckDB-from-pandas idiom. con = duckdb.connect(); con.register("df", pandas_df); result = con.execute("SELECT ... FROM df").df() — zero-copy round trip. Use for window functions and joins you'd rather express in SQL. Polars-from-DuckDB idiom. duckdb.query("SELECT ... FROM 'file.parquet'").pl() — zero-copy. Use this when reading file formats DuckDB handles better (Iceberg, CSV with messy delimiters). The "join in DuckDB" rule. Three-way joins and above are almost always faster in DuckDB than in pandas / Polars because of the cost-based optimiser. If your pipeline has 2+ joins, run them through DuckDB. Out-of-core groupby in Polars. pl.scan_parquet(...).group_by(...).agg(...).collect(streaming=True) handles inputs larger than RAM by spilling intermediates to $POLARS_TMP_DIR (default /tmp ). Set this to a fast SSD path for big jobs. Out-of-core groupby in DuckDB. PRAGMA memory_limit='12GB'; PRAGMA temp_directory='/mnt/nvme/duckdb-tmp'; — DuckDB will spill to the temp_directory when the in-memory hash table exceeds the limit. Always set both pragmas in production. The Arrow zero-copy ladder. Pandas (Arrow-backed) ↔ Polars ↔ DuckDB ↔ PyArrow Table — all conversions zero-copy when the underlying buffers are Arrow. Only conversion to NumPy / Sklearn costs a real copy, and only for primitive dtypes. Polars vs pandas migration. Replace pd.read_* with pl.scan_* , replace df["x"] with pl.col("x") , replace df.groupby("k")["v"].sum() with df.group_by("k").agg(pl.col("v").sum()) . Most pandas pipelines translate in a few hours. DuckDB SQL on a Polars DataFrame. polars_df.sql("SELECT * FROM self WHERE country = 'US'") — Polars ships a built-in SQL frontend backed by DuckDB. Useful for adding a SQL hatch without import duckdb . Frequently asked questions Is Polars faster than pandas? Yes — typically 5-10x faster on the same workload, sometimes 20x+ on large groupby / join pipelines. The structural reasons: Polars's Rust core drops the GIL completely (true multi-threading), the lazy execution model rewrites the plan with predicate / projection pushdown, and the Arrow columnar layout makes vectorised kernels faster than pandas's row-friendly NumPy backend. pandas 2.0 with the PyArrow backend narrows the gap to ~2-3x, but Polars still wins on every workload where multi-threading matters (groupby, join, sort, large filters). The polars vs pandas choice is structural, not implementation polish — polars benchmark results have been consistent across versions since Polars 0.20. Polars vs DuckDB — which should I learn first? Both, but in this order: DuckDB first if you are already SQL-fluent; Polars first if you are coming from pandas. DuckDB lets a SQL-fluent engineer write production-grade pipelines in days — the SQL dialect is mostly Postgres-compatible and the cost-based optimiser does the heavy lifting. Polars is the right answer for column-shaping-heavy workloads (per-user windows, derived columns, expression chains) where the expression API beats SQL on readability. In practice, senior data engineers use both: Polars for shaping and DuckDB for joins, with arrow columnar interop tying them together in one Python process. Does pandas 2.0 close the gap to Polars? Partially. pandas 2.0 's PyArrow backend ( dtype_backend="pyarrow" ) gives 2-4x memory savings and 2-3x speed improvements on string-heavy workloads. Combined with copy_on_write mode, it eliminates many of the silent-copy bugs that plagued earlier pandas. But it does not close the gap on parallelism (pandas is still single-threaded), lazy evaluation (pandas is still eager), or memory-bound workloads (no streaming engine). Polars is still 3-5x faster than Arrow-backed pandas on most workloads. The pandas 2.0 upgrade is worth doing immediately — it is one config line per read_* call. But it does not make Polars unnecessary; it makes the migration easier because both engines now share Arrow under the hood. Can I use Polars, pandas, and DuckDB together? Yes — and that is the senior pattern. All three engines share Arrow buffers as their in-memory format, so conversion between them is zero-copy. The typical hybrid pipeline: read + shape in Polars ( scan_parquet → filter → with_columns ), join in DuckDB ( con.register("df", polars_df) then SQL), and only convert to pandas at the edges where Sklearn / Matplotlib / Statsmodels demands it. Arrow ties them together; the conversion functions ( pl.from_pandas , con.from_df , polars_df.to_pandas(use_pyarrow_extension_array=True) ) are zero-copy on Arrow-backed data. The result is a pipeline that uses each engine for what it does best. What is a LazyFrame in Polars? A polars lazy frame is a description of a query — operations, expressions, sources — without any data. You build it with pl.scan_* and chain operations like a regular DataFrame, but nothing executes until you call .collect() or .fetch() . Before execution, the optimiser walks the plan and applies predicate pushdown (filters moved to the data source), projection pushdown (only required columns read), common-subexpression elimination (shared expressions computed once), slice pushdown (head / tail moved to the source), and join reordering (build / probe side picked optimally). The result is often 10-100x less I/O than the equivalent eager read_* + post-filter pipeline. LazyFrames are the single biggest reason Polars beats pandas on large workloads — the engine sees the whole plan and can optimise it before running. Is Polars production-ready in 2026? Yes. Polars 1.0 shipped in 2024 and stabilised the API; Polars 1.x is now the default for new data-engineering pipelines at most modern teams. The streaming engine handles out-of-core workloads, the Python wheels are universally installable (Linux, macOS, Windows, ARM), and the integration story with the broader Python ecosystem (PyArrow, DuckDB, Sklearn via pandas conversion) is mature. The remaining gaps versus pandas are in ecosystem libraries — some Sklearn / Statsmodels / SciPy functions still expect pandas DataFrames, so the senior pattern is to convert at those edges. For 95% of production data pipelines in 2026, Polars is a safe default; pandas remains the right choice only at the Sklearn / Matplotlib / SciPy edge and for ad-hoc exploration on < 100MB data. Practice on PipeCode Drill the aggregation practice library → for the groupby / window / pivot family of probes. Rehearse on data-manipulation drills → for the column-shaping + expression idioms. Sharpen the optimization library → for the query-plan + memory-budget probes. Stack the SQL practice library → when the interviewer probes DuckDB-style window functions and CTEs. Layer the data-structures library → for the columnar-layout + Arrow-backed dtype probes. For end-to-end pipeline design, work through the ETL practice library → . Lock in dataframe-engine muscle memory Library docs explain methods. PipeCode drills explain the decision — when Polars beats pandas, when DuckDB beats Polars on joins, when Arrow zero-copy saves a serialization round-trip. Pipecode.ai is Leetcode for Data Engineering — pattern-first practice tuned for the production trade-offs senior data engineers actually face. Practice aggregation problems → Practice data-manipulation problems →
